skill_tested: Identifying mechanisms of nerve impulse transmission
question_text: During an action potential in a myelinated axon, the rapid jumping of the impulse between nodes of Ranvier is called
answer: saltatory conduction

choices.text: saltatory conduction
continuous conduction
synaptic transmission
refractory summation

explanation: Myelin insulates the axon except at nodes of Ranvier, forcing depolarization to jump between nodes. This saltatory conduction increases conduction speed.
common_mistake: Students confuse saltatory conduction with continuous conduction along unmyelinated fibres.

skill_tested: Predicting effects of ion channel blockers on action potentials
question_text: A drug that blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in axon membranes would most directly prevent
answer: depolarization of the axon membrane

choices.text: depolarization of the axon membrane
reuptake of neurotransmitter
release of acetylcholine from vesicles
hyperpolarization during repolarization

explanation: Voltage-gated sodium channels allow Na$^+$ influx during depolarization. Blocking them stops the rising phase of the action potential.
common_mistake: Students may choose neurotransmitter release, which occurs at synapses rather than along the axon during depolarization.

skill_tested: Explaining maintenance of resting membrane potential
question_text: The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is maintained primarily by
answer: the sodium-potassium pump and selective membrane permeability

choices.text: the sodium-potassium pump and selective membrane permeability
continuous action potentials along dendrites
myelin preventing all ion movement
equal permeability to Na$^+$ and K$^+$

explanation: The Na$^+$/K$^+$ pump actively transports ions while the membrane is more permeable to K$^+$ at rest, maintaining a negative interior near $-70$ mV.
common_mistake: Students sometimes attribute resting potential to myelin alone or to equal ion permeability.

skill_tested: Identifying ion movements during action potential phases
question_text: Which event occurs immediately after the peak of an action potential during repolarization?
answer: Potassium ions move out of the axon

choices.text: Potassium ions move out of the axon
Sodium ions move into the axon
Calcium ions enter the synaptic knob
Neurotransmitter is released into the synaptic cleft

explanation: During repolarization, voltage-gated K$^+$ channels open and K$^+$ exits the axon, restoring the negative membrane potential.
common_mistake: Sodium influx occurs during depolarization, not repolarization.

skill_tested: Applying enzyme inhibition concepts to synaptic transmission
question_text: A competitive inhibitor at a synapse binds to the active site of an enzyme that breaks down neurotransmitter. The most likely effect is
answer: prolonged presence of neurotransmitter in the synaptic cleft

choices.text: prolonged presence of neurotransmitter in the synaptic cleft
faster breakdown of neurotransmitter
blocked release of neurotransmitter from vesicles
prevented binding of neurotransmitter to receptors

explanation: Blocking the enzyme that degrades neurotransmitter (such as acetylcholinesterase) leaves more neurotransmitter available in the cleft, prolonging the signal.
common_mistake: Students may confuse competitive enzyme inhibition with receptor blocking.

skill_tested: Explaining functional significance of refractory periods
question_text: The absolute refractory period of a neuron ensures that
answer: action potentials travel in one direction along the axon

choices.text: action potentials travel in one direction along the axon
the neuron can fire continuously without limit
neurotransmitters are always excitatory
the resting potential becomes more positive over time

explanation: During the absolute refractory period, voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels cannot reopen, preventing backward propagation and limiting firing frequency.
common_mistake: Students may think the refractory period increases firing rate rather than limiting it.

skill_tested: Relating myelin damage to physiological symptoms
question_text: A patient with damaged myelin sheaths on peripheral sensory neurons would most likely experience
answer: slower nerve impulse conduction

choices.text: slower nerve impulse conduction
inability to produce neurotransmitters
loss of resting membrane potential
elimination of all reflex arcs

explanation: Myelin increases conduction velocity via saltatory conduction. Demyelination slows impulse transmission along affected neurons.
common_mistake: Myelin damage affects conduction speed, not the fundamental ability to maintain resting potential.

skill_tested: Describing events at a chemical synapse
question_text: At a chemical synapse, the arrival of an action potential at the presynaptic terminal triggers
answer: calcium influx and vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane

choices.text: calcium influx and vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane
sodium influx into the postsynaptic neuron only
immediate electrical current flow through gap junctions
breakdown of receptors on the postsynaptic membrane

explanation: Depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca$^{2+}$ channels; calcium entry causes synaptic vesicles to fuse and release neurotransmitter.
common_mistake: Students may confuse electrical synapses (gap junctions) with chemical synapses requiring vesicle release.

question_text: An excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP) moves the membrane potential
answer: closer to threshold

choices.text: closer to threshold
below the resting potential further
to exactly the resting potential always
above threshold in every case

explanation: EPSPs are graded depolarizations that bring the membrane closer to the $-55$ mV threshold needed to trigger an action potential.
common_mistake: Students may assume every EPSP always produces a full action potential regardless of summation.

question_text: Temporal summation occurs when
answer: multiple EPSPs from the same synapse arrive in rapid succession

choices.text: multiple EPSPs from the same synapse arrive in rapid succession
EPSPs from different synapses arrive at the same time
inhibitory signals cancel all excitatory signals
myelin is removed from the axon

explanation: Temporal summation integrates signals from one synapse over a short time window; spatial summation integrates signals from multiple synapses simultaneously.
common_mistake: Spatial summation from different synapses is often confused with temporal summation.

skill_tested: Extracting numerical values from membrane potential data
question_text: A neuron has a resting membrane potential of $-72$ mV and reaches a peak depolarization of $+35$ mV during an action potential. Express the resting potential as a two-digit positive value (ignore the sign) and the peak as a two-digit positive value. Record all four digits of your answer.
explanation: Resting potential $-72$ mV gives digits $72$. Peak depolarization $+35$ mV gives digits $35$. Combined answer: $7235$.
common_mistake: Students may include negative signs or reverse the order of the two values.

skill_tested: Sequencing events in a monosynaptic reflex arc
question_text: During a knee-jerk reflex, place the following events in the correct chronological order: (1) motor neuron fires, (2) muscle contracts, (3) stretch receptor detects change, (4) sensory impulse reaches spinal cord. Record all four digits of your answer.
explanation: The stretch receptor fires first (3), the sensory signal reaches the spinal cord (4), the motor neuron responds (1), and the effector muscle contracts (2).
common_mistake: Students often place muscle contraction before motor neuron activation.

question_text: The correct pathway through a reflex arc is
answer: receptor $\rightarrow$ sensory neuron $\rightarrow$ interneuron $\rightarrow$ motor neuron $\rightarrow$ effector

choices.text: receptor $\rightarrow$ sensory neuron $\rightarrow$ interneuron $\rightarrow$ motor neuron $\rightarrow$ effector
effector $\rightarrow$ motor neuron $\rightarrow$ sensory neuron $\rightarrow$ receptor
sensory neuron $\rightarrow$ receptor $\rightarrow$ interneuron $\rightarrow$ effector
receptor $\rightarrow$ motor neuron $\rightarrow$ sensory neuron $\rightarrow$ effector

explanation: A reflex arc begins with a receptor detecting a stimulus, continues through sensory and interneurons in the CNS, and ends with a motor neuron activating an effector.
common_mistake: The direction of impulse flow in reflex arcs is commonly reversed.

skill_tested: Explaining spinal reflex processing vs conscious perception
question_text: Withdrawal from a hot surface before pain is consciously perceived demonstrates that
answer: interneurons in the spinal cord can process reflexes without cerebrum involvement

choices.text: interneurons in the spinal cord can process reflexes without cerebrum involvement
pain receptors do not send signals to the brain
the parasympathetic system controls voluntary movement
motor neurons originate in the cerebrum for all reflexes

explanation: Spinal reflex arcs allow rapid motor responses processed locally; conscious pain perception follows separately via ascending pathways.
common_mistake: Students may think the brain must process all responses before any movement occurs.

skill_tested: Comparing somatic and autonomic nervous systems
question_text: The autonomic nervous system differs from the somatic nervous system because autonomic pathways
answer: involuntarily regulate glands and smooth muscle

choices.text: involuntarily regulate glands and smooth muscle
only control skeletal muscle for voluntary movement
do not use motor neurons
function only in the peripheral nervous system without CNS input

explanation: The autonomic division controls involuntary functions via two-neuron chains to cardiac muscle, smooth muscle, and glands.
common_mistake: Voluntary skeletal muscle control is a somatic, not autonomic, function.

question_text: Stimulation of the sympathetic division during exercise would most likely cause
answer: increased heart rate and decreased blood flow to digestive organs

choices.text: increased heart rate and decreased blood flow to digestive organs
decreased heart rate and increased digestion
pupil constriction and increased salivation
decreased blood glucose and bronchial constriction

explanation: Sympathetic activation prepares the body for activity: heart rate rises, blood shifts from digestion to muscles, pupils dilate, and glycogenolysis increases blood glucose.
common_mistake: Parasympathetic responses are often incorrectly attributed to sympathetic stimulation.

question_text: The parasympathetic nervous system is sometimes called the 'rest and digest' system because it
answer: promotes digestion and conserves energy

choices.text: promotes digestion and conserves energy
prepares the body for fight-or-flight responses
increases heart rate during stress
dilates bronchioles for maximum oxygen intake

explanation: Parasympathetic activity dominates during rest, stimulating digestion, reducing heart rate, and promoting energy storage.
common_mistake: Fight-or-flight responses belong to the sympathetic division.

question_text: The hypothalamus coordinates both nervous and endocrine responses primarily by
answer: regulating the pituitary gland and autonomic centres

choices.text: regulating the pituitary gland and autonomic centres
producing all hormones in the body directly
filtering blood before it reaches the brain
generating conscious voluntary movement

explanation: The hypothalamus links the nervous and endocrine systems by controlling pituitary hormone release and autonomic function.
common_mistake: Students may think the hypothalamus produces every hormone or controls voluntary skeletal movement.

skill_tested: Matching brain regions to physiological functions
question_text: Damage to the cerebellum would most likely impair
answer: coordination and balance

choices.text: coordination and balance
regulation of breathing rate
interpretation of visual images
long-term memory formation

explanation: The cerebellum fine-tunes motor coordination, balance, and posture. Breathing is regulated by the medulla; vision by the occipital lobe.
common_mistake: Brain structure functions are frequently confused on diploma-style questions.

question_text: The medulla oblongata is essential for survival because it controls
answer: involuntary functions such as breathing and heart rate

choices.text: involuntary functions such as breathing and heart rate
conscious decision-making and reasoning
storage of procedural memories only
secretion of all digestive enzymes

explanation: The medulla contains vital centres for respiration, cardiovascular regulation, and other autonomic functions.
common_mistake: Conscious reasoning is a cerebrum function, not medulla.

skill_tested: Associating cerebrum lobes with sensory processing
question_text: The occipital lobe of the cerebrum is primarily responsible for processing
answer: visual information

choices.text: visual information
auditory signals
touch and temperature
voluntary motor commands

explanation: Primary visual cortex in the occipital lobe interprets signals from the retina. Auditory processing occurs in the temporal lobe.
common_mistake: Students confuse occipital (vision) with temporal (hearing) lobe functions.

question_text: Rods and cones are located in the
answer: retina

choices.text: retina
cornea
lens
optic nerve

explanation: Photoreceptors (rods for low light, cones for colour) are embedded in the retinal layer at the back of the eye.
common_mistake: The lens focuses light; photoreceptors detect it in the retina.

question_text: Cones are concentrated in the fovea and are most responsible for
answer: colour vision and high visual acuity in bright light

choices.text: colour vision and high visual acuity in bright light
night vision in very dim light
detecting only black and white without detail
maintaining balance during head movement

explanation: Cones detect colour and fine detail in bright conditions; rods dominate peripheral and low-light vision.
common_mistake: Rods, not cones, are primarily responsible for dim-light vision.

skill_tested: Defining optical processes in the eye
question_text: The bending of light as it passes through the cornea and lens is called
answer: refraction

choices.text: refraction
accommodation only
transduction
depolarization

explanation: Refraction changes the path of light to focus an image on the retina. Accommodation specifically refers to lens shape changes for near focus.
common_mistake: Transduction refers to converting light to neural signals in photoreceptors.

question_text: After light passes through the lens, it strikes photoreceptors in the correct order through these structures: lens $\rightarrow$ vitreous humour $\rightarrow$ retina. Which structure converts light energy into neural impulses?
answer: photoreceptors in the retina

choices.text: photoreceptors in the retina
the cornea
the iris
the optic nerve sheath

explanation: Photoreceptors in the retina transduce light into electrochemical signals that travel via bipolar and ganglion cells to the optic nerve.
common_mistake: The optic nerve transmits signals but does not perform initial transduction.

question_text: The semicircular canals of the inner ear detect
answer: rotational acceleration of the head

choices.text: rotational acceleration of the head
sound frequency and pitch
linear air pressure changes only
blood pressure in cerebral arteries

explanation: Semicircular canals contain fluid and hair cells that detect angular head movements for dynamic equilibrium.
common_mistake: Sound detection occurs in the cochlea, not semicircular canals.

question_text: Sound vibrations are converted to neural impulses primarily in the
answer: organ of Corti within the cochlea

choices.text: organ of Corti within the cochlea
tympanic membrane only
auditory canal only
ossicles without cochlear involvement

explanation: Mechanical vibrations are transmitted through ossicles to the cochlea, where hair cells in the organ of Corti transduce them into nerve impulses.
common_mistake: The eardrum vibrates but does not generate neural impulses directly.

question_text: The ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) function to
answer: amplify and transmit vibrations from the eardrum to the cochlea

choices.text: amplify and transmit vibrations from the eardrum to the cochlea
produce cerebrospinal fluid for the brain
detect changes in head position
filter blood entering the inner ear

explanation: Ossicles form a lever system that amplifies sound vibrations and delivers them to the oval window of the cochlea.
common_mistake: Ossicles transmit sound; they do not detect balance.

skill_tested: Sequencing structures in the auditory pathway
question_text: Place the following auditory pathway structures in the order sound travels through them from the environment to the cochlea: (1) tympanic membrane, (2) auditory canal, (3) cochlea, (4) ossicles. Record all four digits.
explanation: Sound enters the auditory canal (2), strikes the tympanic membrane (4), passes through ossicles (1), and reaches the cochlea (3).
common_mistake: Students may place the cochlea before the ossicles.

question_text: Taste receptors on the tongue are examples of
answer: chemoreceptors

choices.text: chemoreceptors
photoreceptors
mechanoreceptors
nociceptors

explanation: Taste receptors detect dissolved chemical stimuli, classifying them as chemoreceptors.
common_mistake: Mechanoreceptors respond to pressure; photoreceptors to light.

question_text: Adaptation of sensory receptors explains why a person stops noticing the feeling of a watch on the wrist after several minutes because
answer: receptor firing frequency decreases despite constant stimulus

choices.text: receptor firing frequency decreases despite constant stimulus
the stimulus is no longer present
motor neurons stop functioning
the brain permanently deletes the sensory pathway

explanation: Sensory adaptation reduces perceived intensity when a stimulus remains constant, even though the stimulus persists.
common_mistake: Students may think the stimulus itself disappears rather than neural response adapting.

question_text: The minimum stimulus intensity required to trigger a nerve impulse is called the
answer: threshold

choices.text: threshold
refractory period
resting potential
synaptic delay

explanation: Threshold is the level of depolarization (about $-55$ mV) needed to initiate an action potential.
common_mistake: Resting potential is the baseline state, not the trigger level.

skill_tested: Predicting visual field defects from pathway damage
question_text: A patient with a lesion affecting the optic chiasm may lose vision in the
answer: temporal (lateral) visual fields of both eyes

choices.text: temporal (lateral) visual fields of both eyes
entire left eye only
entire right eye only
central foveal vision only

explanation: At the optic chiasm, nasal retinal fibres cross. Damage here classically produces bitemporal hemianopia.
common_mistake: Complete monocular blindness suggests damage posterior to the chiasm on one side.

question_text: Glial cells in the nervous system are important because they
answer: support, insulate, and protect neurons

choices.text: support, insulate, and protect neurons
transmit action potentials to muscles directly
secrete all hormones of the endocrine system
replace neurons after every action potential

explanation: Glial cells (oligodendrocytes, Schwann cells, astrocytes, microglia) maintain the environment for neurons, including myelination and support.
common_mistake: Neurons, not glia, conduct action potentials.

skill_tested: Applying myelin biology to disease context
question_text: Multiple sclerosis involves immune attack on myelin in the CNS. Affected neurons would show
answer: decreased conduction velocity and possible signal loss

choices.text: decreased conduction velocity and possible signal loss
increased neurotransmitter production in all synapses
permanent inability to maintain ATP levels
conversion of sensory neurons to motor neurons

explanation: Demyelination disrupts saltatory conduction, slowing or blocking impulses along CNS pathways.
common_mistake: MS affects myelin, not the fundamental identity of neuron types.

question_text: Which neurotransmitter is most directly associated with neuromuscular junction transmission to skeletal muscle?
answer: acetylcholine

choices.text: acetylcholine
dopamine
serotonin
gABA

explanation: Motor neurons release acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, binding nicotinic receptors on muscle fibres.
common_mistake: Dopamine and serotonin are primarily CNS neurotransmitters, not the main NMJ transmitter.

skill_tested: Predicting autonomic drug effects on heart rate
question_text: A muscarinic antagonist blocking acetylcholine receptors on the sinoatrial node would most likely
answer: increase heart rate by reducing parasympathetic slowing

choices.text: increase heart rate by reducing parasympathetic slowing
decrease heart rate by enhancing parasympathetic tone
block all pain perception permanently
increase digestive enzyme secretion via sympathetic nerves

explanation: Parasympathetic ACh slows the SA node; blocking muscarinic receptors reduces this braking effect, allowing heart rate to rise.
common_mistake: Students confuse receptor blockade effects with agonist stimulation.

question_text: Hormones differ from neurotransmitters in that hormones typically
answer: travel through the bloodstream to reach target cells

choices.text: travel through the bloodstream to reach target cells
only function across synaptic clefts
are always lipid-insoluble and cannot enter cells
are released only by neurons in the spinal cord

explanation: Endocrine hormones are secreted into blood and act on distant targets, whereas neurotransmitters act locally across synapses.
common_mistake: Some hormones are lipid-soluble and can enter cells; the key distinction is transport distance and mechanism.

question_text: The anterior pituitary is classified as a true endocrine gland because it
answer: secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream

choices.text: secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream
only stores hormones made by the hypothalamus without secretion
connects directly to neurons in the cerebrum
produces only digestive enzymes

explanation: The anterior pituitary synthesizes and releases tropic hormones (TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, GH, prolactin) into circulation.
common_mistake: Hormone storage without secretion describes the posterior pituitary's role for ADH and oxytocin.

skill_tested: Explaining negative feedback in endocrine regulation
question_text: Negative feedback in hormone regulation means that
answer: rising hormone levels suppress further hormone release

choices.text: rising hormone levels suppress further hormone release
hormone levels always increase without limit
target organs stop responding after one signal
the nervous system is not involved in any hormonal control

explanation: Negative feedback loops (e.g., thyroid hormone inhibiting TSH) maintain homeostasis by counteracting changes.
common_mistake: Positive feedback (e.g., LH surge) is confused with the more common negative feedback.

question_text: In the regulation of blood glucose, insulin and glucagon demonstrate
answer: antagonistic hormonal control

choices.text: antagonistic hormonal control
identical effects on all target tissues
positive feedback without set point
control only by the sympathetic nervous system alone

explanation: Insulin lowers blood glucose by promoting uptake and storage; glucagon raises it by stimulating glycogenolysis — opposing actions.
common_mistake: Both hormones are endocrine, not purely sympathetic responses.

skill_tested: Distinguishing Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes mechanisms
question_text: Type 1 diabetes results primarily from
answer: destruction of pancreatic beta cells and insufficient insulin secretion

choices.text: destruction of pancreatic beta cells and insufficient insulin secretion
target cell resistance to insulin only
excessive glucagon production with normal insulin
overproduction of insulin causing hypoglycemia

explanation: Type 1 is autoimmune beta-cell destruction leading to insulin deficiency. Type 2 involves insulin resistance.
common_mistake: Insulin resistance characterizes Type 2, not Type 1 diabetes.

skill_tested: Relating iodine to thyroid hormone synthesis
question_text: The thyroid gland requires dietary iodine because iodine is used to synthesize
answer: thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3)

choices.text: thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3)
insulin and glucagon
adrenaline and noradrenaline
estrogen and progesterone

explanation: Thyroid hormones contain iodine atoms. Iodine deficiency impairs T3/T4 production and can cause goiter.
common_mistake: Adrenal catecholamines and pancreatic hormones do not require iodine.

question_text: A person with hypothyroidism would most likely experience
answer: decreased metabolic rate and fatigue

choices.text: decreased metabolic rate and fatigue
excessive sweating and rapid weight loss
chronically elevated blood calcium
hyperglycemia from excess insulin

explanation: Low thyroid hormone reduces metabolic rate, causing fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain.
common_mistake: Hyperthyroid symptoms (weight loss, heat intolerance) are often reversed in hypo cases.

skill_tested: Connecting adrenal medulla to stress response
question_text: Adrenaline (epinephrine) release during stress is an example of
answer: a rapid hormonal response coordinated with the sympathetic nervous system

choices.text: a rapid hormonal response coordinated with the sympathetic nervous system
parasympathetic rest-and-digest activity
a long-term growth response mediated by GH only
negative feedback that lowers blood glucose immediately

explanation: The adrenal medulla releases adrenaline during sympathetic activation, quickly increasing heart rate and blood glucose.
common_mistake: Adrenaline raises, not lowers, blood glucose during stress.

question_text: Cortisol secretion by the adrenal cortex helps the body respond to prolonged stress by
answer: mobilizing energy reserves and suppressing non-essential immune responses

choices.text: mobilizing energy reserves and suppressing non-essential immune responses
permanently increasing bone density
stimulating immediate pupil constriction
blocking all action potentials in sensory neurons

explanation: Cortisol increases gluconeogenesis and modulates immune function during chronic stress.
common_mistake: Pupil constriction is not a primary cortisol effect.

skill_tested: Comparing steroid and peptide hormone mechanisms
question_text: Steroid hormones such as cortisol exert their effects by
answer: binding intracellular receptors and altering gene transcription

choices.text: binding intracellular receptors and altering gene transcription
binding only ion-channel receptors on the cell surface
functioning exclusively as second messengers in the cytoplasm
digesting target cell DNA directly

explanation: Lipid-soluble steroid hormones enter cells and bind receptors that regulate gene expression.
common_mistake: Peptide hormones typically use membrane receptors and second messengers.

question_text: Peptide hormones such as insulin typically act through
answer: membrane receptors and second messenger pathways

choices.text: membrane receptors and second messenger pathways
direct diffusion through the membrane without receptors
binding DNA in the nucleus without intermediates
permanent alteration of the cell's chromosome number

explanation: Insulin binds cell-surface receptors, triggering phosphorylation cascades (second messengers) without entering the nucleus directly.
common_mistake: Steroid hormone mechanisms are incorrectly applied to peptide hormones.

question_text: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis regulates cortisol through
answer: CRH from hypothalamus $\rightarrow$ ACTH from pituitary $\rightarrow$ cortisol from adrenal cortex

choices.text: CRH from hypothalamus $\rightarrow$ ACTH from pituitary $\rightarrow$ cortisol from adrenal cortex
TSH from pituitary $\rightarrow$ insulin from pancreas
ADH from posterior pituitary $\rightarrow$ aldosterone from liver
FSH from adrenal $\rightarrow$ estrogen from hypothalamus

explanation: The HPA axis uses CRH and ACTH as tropic signals culminating in cortisol release, with negative feedback at each level.
common_mistake: TSH and insulin belong to different endocrine axes.

skill_tested: Explaining aldosterone role in fluid balance
question_text: Aldosterone acts on kidney tubules to
answer: increase sodium reabsorption and water retention

choices.text: increase sodium reabsorption and water retention
decrease blood volume during dehydration
excrete all potassium regardless of body needs
block ADH release from the hypothalamus

explanation: Aldosterone promotes Na$^+$ reabsorption, indirectly conserving water and maintaining blood pressure.
common_mistake: Aldosterone conserves sodium and water; it does not promote dehydration.

question_text: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) is released when blood osmolarity increases. Its primary effect is to
answer: increase water reabsorption in the collecting ducts of the kidney

choices.text: increase water reabsorption in the collecting ducts of the kidney
stimulate glucose release from the liver only
decrease sweat production in the skin exclusively
increase filtration rate in the glomerulus without reabsorption

explanation: ADH inserts aquaporins in collecting duct cells, increasing water reabsorption and concentrating urine.
common_mistake: ADH targets water reabsorption, not primarily hepatic glucose release.

skill_tested: Explaining PTH role in calcium homeostasis
question_text: Calcium homeostasis involves parathyroid hormone (PTH), which
answer: increases blood calcium by stimulating bone resorption and renal reabsorption

choices.text: increases blood calcium by stimulating bone resorption and renal reabsorption
lowers blood calcium by depositing it in bone exclusively
functions identically to calcitonin from the thyroid
blocks vitamin D production permanently

explanation: PTH raises blood Ca$^{2+}$ by releasing calcium from bone and increasing renal reabsorption. Calcitonin lowers blood calcium.
common_mistake: PTH and calcitonin have opposing effects on blood calcium.

question_text: A negative feedback loop maintaining body temperature on a cold day involves
answer: shivering increasing heat production until temperature rises to set point

choices.text: shivering increasing heat production until temperature rises to set point
continuous shivering regardless of core temperature
sweating to cool the body while core temperature drops
permanent shutdown of the hypothalamus

explanation: Thermoregulation uses negative feedback: cold triggers shivering; when core temperature returns to set point, shivering decreases.
common_mistake: Sweating cools the body during overheating, not during cold exposure.

question_text: A patient has a resting heart rate of $68$ beats/min before exercise and $142$ beats/min immediately after sprinting. Record the resting rate as a two-digit value followed by the exercise rate as a three-digit value (all five digits).
explanation: Resting rate $68$ is recorded as $68$. Exercise rate $142$ is recorded as $142$. Combined: $68142$.
common_mistake: Students may record digits without padding or reverse the order.

question_text: Diabetes insipidus involving insufficient ADH would cause
answer: excessive dilute urine production and dehydration risk

choices.text: excessive dilute urine production and dehydration risk
concentrated urine with low water loss
complete inability to filter blood in kidneys
permanent hyperglycemia identical to diabetes mellitus

explanation: Without ADH, collecting ducts reabsorb little water, producing large volumes of dilute urine.
common_mistake: Diabetes insipidus affects water balance, not blood glucose like diabetes mellitus.

skill_tested: Applying lock-and-key model to hormone-receptor binding
question_text: The lock-and-key model best describes how
answer: a hormone binds specifically to its receptor

choices.text: a hormone binds specifically to its receptor
all hormones enter all cells without specificity
DNA replicates during mitosis
action potentials jump between any adjacent cells

explanation: Hormone-receptor binding is highly specific, like a key fitting a particular lock.
common_mistake: The lock-and-key model applies to molecular binding, not action potential propagation.

question_text: Up-regulation of hormone receptors on target cells would most likely occur when
answer: hormone concentration has been chronically low

choices.text: hormone concentration has been chronically low
hormone concentration is chronically very high
the target tissue is permanently removed
all feedback loops are eliminated

explanation: Cells increase receptor number (up-regulation) when hormone signals are weak; down-regulation occurs with chronic excess.
common_mistake: Chronic high hormone levels typically cause down-regulation, not up-regulation.

skill_tested: Identifying positive feedback in endocrine regulation
question_text: Oxytocin release during childbirth is an example of
answer: positive feedback amplifying uterine contractions

choices.text: positive feedback amplifying uterine contractions
negative feedback maintaining constant hormone levels
a reflex arc involving only somatic motor neurons
hormonal control without any nervous system involvement

explanation: Oxytocin stimulates stronger contractions, which trigger more oxytocin — a positive feedback loop until birth completes.
common_mistake: Most endocrine homeostasis uses negative, not positive, feedback.

question_text: Growth hormone (GH) from the anterior pituitary directly stimulates
answer: protein synthesis and cell growth in many tissues

choices.text: protein synthesis and cell growth in many tissues
melanin production in skin only
sperm production in the testes without any intermediates
reabsorption of glucose in the kidney exclusively

explanation: GH promotes growth via direct effects and by stimulating IGF-1 production in the liver.
common_mistake: GH has broad metabolic effects, not limited to a single tissue.

skill_tested: Explaining goiter formation from iodine deficiency
question_text: A goiter visible as neck swelling may develop when dietary iodine is insufficient because
answer: TSH stimulation increases thyroid gland size while hormone synthesis remains low

choices.text: TSH stimulation increases thyroid gland size while hormone synthesis remains low
the pituitary stops producing all tropic hormones
the adrenal cortex overproduces cortisol
parathyroid hormone destroys thyroid follicles

explanation: Low T3/T4 triggers increased TSH, which hypertrophies the thyroid (goiter) without adequate hormone output.
common_mistake: Goiter is thyroid enlargement from TSH overstimulation, not adrenal dysfunction.

skill_tested: Tracing origin of posterior pituitary hormones
question_text: The posterior pituitary stores and releases hormones that are actually synthesized in the
answer: hypothalamus

choices.text: hypothalamus
anterior pituitary
thyroid gland
adrenal medulla

explanation: ADH and oxytocin are produced by hypothalamic neurons and transported axonally to the posterior pituitary for release.
common_mistake: Anterior pituitary synthesizes its own hormones; posterior pituitary only stores hypothalamic hormones.

question_text: Homeostasis in the human body is best defined as
answer: maintenance of stable internal conditions despite external changes

choices.text: maintenance of stable internal conditions despite external changes
elimination of all hormonal feedback loops
keeping all body variables identical at all times without fluctuation
preventing any response to environmental stimuli

explanation: Homeostasis involves dynamic regulation around set points, not rigid constancy with zero fluctuation.
common_mistake: Homeostasis requires feedback responses, not elimination of all change.

skill_tested: Integrating nervous and endocrine glucose regulation
question_text: Describe how the nervous and endocrine systems work together to regulate blood glucose levels after a person eats a large carbohydrate meal. Include at least one hormone, one gland, and one nervous system component in your answer.
answer: After eating, blood glucose rises. Beta cells in the pancreas detect the increase and secrete insulin. Insulin promotes glucose uptake into cells and storage as glycogen in liver and muscle, lowering blood glucose. The parasympathetic nervous system supports digestive activity during the meal. If glucose drops too low later, alpha cells release glucagon, stimulating glycogen breakdown and gluconeogenesis. The hypothalamus monitors energy status and coordinates autonomic responses. Negative feedback on insulin and glucagon maintains glucose near the set point.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Identify blood glucose rise after eating — 1 mark. (2) Name insulin, pancreatic beta cells, and a target effect (uptake/storage) — 2 marks. (3) Include glucagon or counter-regulation — 1 mark. (4) Link nervous system component (parasympathetic or hypothalamic monitoring) — 1 mark. (5) Reference negative feedback/homeostasis — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students describe only insulin without mentioning feedback, or omit nervous system involvement entirely.

question_text: Explain the events of synaptic transmission at a cholinergic synapse, from the arrival of an action potential at the presynaptic terminal to the response in the postsynaptic cell. Your answer should include at least four distinct steps.
answer: An action potential arrives at the presynaptic terminal, depolarizing the membrane. Voltage-gated calcium channels open and Ca$^{2+}$ enters the terminal. Calcium triggers synaptic vesicles containing acetylcholine to fuse with the presynaptic membrane, releasing ACh into the synaptic cleft by exocytosis. ACh diffuses across the cleft and binds to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane. Binding opens ion channels, producing an EPSP or IPSP. Acetylcholinesterase in the cleft breaks down ACh to prevent continuous stimulation. Choline is recycled into the presynaptic neuron.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Ca$^{2+}$ influx and vesicle fusion/exocytosis — 2 marks. (2) ACh diffusion and receptor binding — 1 mark. (3) Postsynaptic potential change — 1 mark. (4) Enzymatic breakdown/recycling — 1 mark. Clear sequence earns communication mark.
common_mistake: Students skip calcium's role or fail to mention termination of the signal by enzymatic breakdown.

question_text: Compare the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions of the autonomic nervous system in terms of general function, one physiological effect on the heart, and one physiological effect on digestion.
answer: Both divisions are part of the autonomic nervous system controlling involuntary functions. Sympathetic: fight-or-flight — increases heart rate, dilates bronchioles, diverts blood from digestion to muscles, inhibits digestive secretions. Parasympathetic: rest-and-digest — decreases heart rate, constricts bronchioles slightly, stimulates digestive gland secretion and peristalsis. The two divisions provide antagonistic control maintaining homeostasis.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) General functional contrast (fight-or-flight vs rest-and-digest) — 2 marks. (2) One correct cardiac effect for each division — 2 marks. (3) One correct digestive effect for each division — 2 marks. Integration or homeostasis comment — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students list effects but do not pair them correctly with the proper division.

question_text: During the pupillary light reflex, order these events: (1) pupil diameter changes, (2) motor neuron depolarizes, (3) light receptors stimulated, (4) sensory neuron depolarizes. Record all four digits.
explanation: Light stimulates receptors (3), sensory neuron fires (4), motor neuron fires (2), pupil changes (1).
common_mistake: Event 1 is the effector response and must come last.

question_text: The all-or-none principle states that once threshold is reached, an action potential of constant magnitude is produced regardless of stimulus strength beyond threshold.
answer: true principle

choices.text: true principle
each stronger stimulus produces a larger action potential
subthreshold stimuli produce partial action potentials
threshold varies randomly for each impulse

question_text: Grey matter in the CNS consists primarily of

myelinated axon tracts only
cerebrospinal fluid reservoirs
collagen connective tissue without neurons

question_text: Match receptor type to stimulus: (1) photoreceptor, (2) mechanoreceptor in cochlea, (3) chemoreceptor for taste, (4) nociceptor. Which receptor detects light? Record the single digit.
explanation: Photoreceptors (1) in the retina detect light energy.

skill_tested: Calculating change in neuronal firing frequency
question_text: A neuron fires at $25$ Hz at rest stimulation and $85$ Hz under strong stimulation. What is the increase in firing frequency in Hz? Record the two-digit answer.
explanation: Increase = $85 - 25 = 60$ Hz.
common_mistake: Students record the final frequency instead of the difference.

question_text: Explain how negative feedback maintains thyroid hormone levels in the body. Include the roles of the hypothalamus, pituitary, thyroid, and at least one hormone in your answer.
answer: The hypothalamus releases TRH, stimulating the anterior pituitary to secrete TSH. TSH stimulates the thyroid to produce T3 and T4. When blood levels of T3/T4 rise sufficiently, they inhibit TRH and TSH release (negative feedback), reducing further thyroid stimulation. If thyroid hormone drops, inhibition decreases and the axis increases production, returning levels toward the set point.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) TRH and TSH named with correct source glands — 2 marks. (2) T3/T4 from thyroid — 1 mark. (3) Negative feedback inhibition described — 2 marks. (4) Dynamic return to set point — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students describe the axis but omit the inhibitory feedback step.

question_text: A researcher measures pupil diameter in dim light ($6$ mm) and bright light ($2$ mm). Explain the reflex pathway responsible for the change and identify which division of the autonomic nervous system mediates the response in bright light.
answer: In bright light, retinal photoreceptors send signals via the optic nerve to brainstem centres that activate parasympathetic neurons. Parasympathetic fibres release acetylcholine at the iris sphincter muscle, causing constriction (pupil diameter decreases from $6$ mm to $2$ mm). This pupillary light reflex protects the retina from excess light. Afferent: optic nerve; efferent: oculomotor parasympathetic pathway.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Sensory input from retina/optic nerve — 1 mark. (2) Brainstem integration — 1 mark. (3) Parasympathetic efferent to iris — 2 marks. (4) Pupil constriction as effector response — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students attribute pupil constriction to sympathetic rather than parasympathetic activation.

question_text: Spermatogenesis occurs in the

skill_tested: Tracing hormonal control of ovarian cycle
question_text: Describe the hormonal regulation of the ovarian cycle from the follicular phase through ovulation. Include the roles of FSH, estrogen, LH, and the corpus luteum.
answer: FSH stimulates follicle growth; developing follicles secrete increasing estrogen. Rising estrogen initially inhibits LH/FSH, then at high levels triggers a positive feedback LH surge causing ovulation. The ruptured follicle becomes the corpus luteum, secreting progesterone and estrogen to maintain the endometrium. If pregnancy does not occur, corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone drops, and a new cycle begins.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) FSH and follicle development — 1 mark. (2) Estrogen rise and feedback — 2 marks. (3) LH surge and ovulation — 2 marks. (4) Corpus luteum progesterone role — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students omit the LH surge or confuse negative and positive feedback timing.

question_text: Explain how cell differentiation and development are influenced by genetic, endocrine, and environmental factors. Provide one specific example for each factor type.
answer: Genetic: Hox genes establish body segment identity along the anterior-posterior axis. Endocrine: maternal and fetal hormones (e.g., testosterone) direct development of reproductive structures. Environmental: teratogens such as alcohol during critical periods can disrupt neural tube closure, causing developmental abnormalities. All three interact — genotype provides the blueprint, hormones regulate timing, and environment can modify expression.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Genetic example with mechanism — 2 marks. (2) Endocrine example — 2 marks. (3) Environmental example — 2 marks. (4) Integration statement — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students provide only one factor type or list factors without specific examples.

question_text: Implantation of the blastocyst normally occurs in the

cervical canal
ovarian follicle wall

explanation: The blastocyst embeds in the uterine endometrium around day 6–7 post-fertilization.
common_mistake: Ectopic pregnancy involves abnormal implantation outside the uterus.

question_text: The umbilical cord connects the fetus to the placenta and contains
answer: umbilical vessels carrying blood between fetus and placenta

choices.text: umbilical vessels carrying blood between fetus and placenta
maternal and fetal blood mixed in one vessel
only nerves without any blood supply
amniotic fluid for fetal breathing

explanation: Two umbilical arteries carry deoxygenated blood to the placenta; one vein returns oxygenated blood. Blood does not mix directly.
common_mistake: Maternal and fetal blood are separated by placental barriers.

question_text: Amniotic fluid functions include
answer: cushioning the fetus and allowing movement for musculoskeletal development

choices.text: cushioning the fetus and allowing movement for musculoskeletal development
providing oxygen through fetal lungs before birth
replacing the placenta in the third trimester
digesting waste in the fetal stomach

explanation: Amniotic fluid protects against mechanical injury and supports development; gas exchange is placental.
common_mistake: Fetal lungs do not function for gas exchange before birth.

question_text: Parturition (birth) is initiated in part by
answer: oxytocin-stimulated uterine contractions with positive feedback

choices.text: oxytocin-stimulated uterine contractions with positive feedback
a permanent drop in all maternal hormones to zero
implantation of the blastocyst in the cervix
completion of spermatogenesis in the fetus

explanation: Oxytocin increases contractions, which stimulate more oxytocin release until delivery.
common_mistake: Hormones coordinate birth; they do not all drop to zero before labour.

question_text: Lactation after birth is maintained primarily by
answer: prolactin stimulating milk production and oxytocin enabling milk ejection

choices.text: prolactin stimulating milk production and oxytocin enabling milk ejection
FSH stimulating milk ducts in the infant
testosterone from the placenta permanently
absence of all pituitary hormones

explanation: Prolactin promotes milk synthesis; oxytocin causes let-down reflex.
common_mistake: FSH does not control lactation.

question_text: A karyotype of a normal human female shows
answer: 22 pairs of autosomes and XX sex chromosomes

choices.text: 22 pairs of autosomes and XX sex chromosomes
22 pairs of autosomes and XY sex chromosomes
23 pairs of autosomes only
44 individual chromosomes without pairing

explanation: Females are 46,XX; males are 46,XY. Karyotypes display homologous pairs.
common_mistake: XY is the male sex chromosome complement.

question_text: Nondisjunction during meiosis can produce gametes with
answer: an abnormal number of chromosomes

choices.text: an abnormal number of chromosomes
always perfectly haploid sets only
no functional genes on any chromosome
mitochondrial DNA only

explanation: Failure of homologues or sister chromatids to separate causes aneuploidy (e.g., trisomy 21).
common_mistake: Nondisjunction causes extra or missing chromosomes, not loss of all nuclear genes.

question_text: Assisted reproductive technology using donor eggs addresses infertility when
answer: the female cannot produce viable eggs but can carry a pregnancy

choices.text: the female cannot produce viable eggs but can carry a pregnancy
the male produces no sperm and no technology can help
both partners have identical genetic disorders requiring no screening
pregnancy is impossible with any uterus present

explanation: Donor oocytes allow gestation when ovarian function is absent but uterus is functional.
common_mistake: Male factor infertility may use donor sperm or ICSI, not only donor eggs.

skill_tested: Analyzing STS issues in reproductive cloning
question_text: Somatic cell nuclear transfer (cloning) raises STS concerns because
answer: it may blur ethical boundaries about personhood and identity

choices.text: it may blur ethical boundaries about personhood and identity
it has been proven to extend human lifespan to 200 years
it requires no oocytes or surrogate uterus ever
it eliminates all genetic variation in populations immediately

explanation: Cloning debates involve identity, consent, and societal implications beyond pure science.
common_mistake: Cloning does not guarantee dramatic lifespan extension.

question_text: Induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells are produced by
answer: reprogramming differentiated cells with specific transcription factors

choices.text: reprogramming differentiated cells with specific transcription factors
fertilizing eggs with multiple sperm simultaneously
removing all DNA from embryonic cells
blocking apoptosis in cancer cells only

explanation: iPS technology resets differentiated cells to a pluripotent state using factors like Oct4 and Sox2.
common_mistake: iPS cells are reprogrammed somatic cells, not products of polyspermy.

question_text: The extraembryonic membrane called the chorion contributes to
answer: placenta formation and production of hCG early in pregnancy

choices.text: placenta formation and production of hCG early in pregnancy
spermatogenesis in the testes
action potential conduction in neurons
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in populations

explanation: Chorionic tissue forms the fetal portion of the placenta and secretes hCG.
common_mistake: Extraembryonic membranes support embryonic development, not gametogenesis in adults.

question_text: Syphilis during pregnancy is a concern because the pathogen can
answer: cross the placenta and infect the fetus

choices.text: cross the placenta and infect the fetus
only affect maternal somatic cells without any fetal contact
cure itself automatically at implantation
stimulate extra chromosome segregation in sperm

explanation: Treponema pallidum can cause congenital syphilis with severe developmental consequences.
common_mistake: Some pathogens cross placental barriers; syphilis is a classic example.

question_text: The process of capacitation of sperm occurs in the
answer: female reproductive tract and enables fertilization ability

choices.text: female reproductive tract and enables fertilization ability
seminiferous tubules before any sperm are produced
placenta after implantation
hypothalamus during puberty

explanation: Sperm must undergo membrane changes in the female tract before they can fertilize an egg.
common_mistake: Sperm are produced inseminiferous tubules but capacitate after ejaculation.

skill_tested: Comparing genetic relatedness of twin types
question_text: Fraternal twins are genetically
answer: as similar as typical siblings (about 50% shared DNA on average)

choices.text: as similar as typical siblings (about 50% shared DNA on average)
100% genetically identical always
unrelated with 0% shared DNA
haploid like gametes

explanation: Dizygotic twins share the same average genetic similarity as ordinary siblings.
common_mistake: 100% identity describes monozygotic twins.

skill_tested: Explaining telomerase role in cell division
question_text: The enzyme telomerase is active in some stem cells and germ cells, which helps by
answer: maintaining chromosome end integrity during repeated divisions

choices.text: maintaining chromosome end integrity during repeated divisions
removing all introns from every mRNA immediately
converting RNA genomes to DNA in all somatic cells
preventing any apoptosis during development

explanation: Telomerase extends telomeres, supporting continued division in stem and germ line cells.
common_mistake: Telomerase does not process mRNA splicing.

skill_tested: Relating maternal environment to fetal development
question_text: Environmental factors such as maternal nutrition during pregnancy can
answer: influence fetal growth and long-term health outcomes

choices.text: influence fetal growth and long-term health outcomes
change the fetal chromosome number in all cells
prevent all gene expression in the embryo
eliminate the need for the placenta

explanation: Nutrient availability affects development and can predispose to adult conditions (developmental origins of health).
common_mistake: Nutrition modulates expression and growth; it does not rewrite karyotype.

question_text: A follicle measures $18$ mm when ovulation-ready and $6$ mm during early follicular phase. What is the increase in diameter in mm? Record the two-digit answer.
explanation: Increase = $18 - 6 = 12$ mm.
common_mistake: Students record final size instead of difference.

question_text: Embryonic development days: fertilization day $0$, implantation day $6$, heartbeat detectable day $22$. Record implantation day as one digit and heartbeat day as two digits (three digits total).
explanation: Implantation = $6$, heartbeat = $22$, combined $622$.
common_mistake: Digit order reversed.

question_text: Compare spermatogenesis and oogenesis in terms of timing of completion, number of functional gametes produced per meiosis, and cytoplasmic distribution.
answer: Spermatogenesis begins at puberty and continues throughout life, producing four functional sperm per primary spermatocyte with equal cytoplasmic division. Oogenesis begins before birth (arrested at prophase I), completes one ovum per month after puberty, yielding one functional ovum and polar bodies due to unequal cytokinesis that concentrates cytoplasm in the egg.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Timing contrast — 2 marks. (2) Gamete number per meiosis — 2 marks. (3) Cytoplasmic distribution — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students claim oogenesis produces four functional gametes like spermatogenesis.

question_text: Discuss one ethical, one economic, and one ecological consideration related to the use of hormonal contraceptives in society.
answer: Ethical: personal reproductive choice vs. cultural or religious objections to artificial hormone use. Economic: cost and accessibility of prescriptions and healthcare compared to potential savings from reduced unintended pregnancies. Ecological: trace hormones in wastewater may affect aquatic organism reproduction, raising environmental monitoring concerns.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Valid ethical consideration — 2 marks. (2) Economic consideration — 2 marks. (3) Ecological consideration — 2 marks. Each must be explained, not just named.
common_mistake: Students list considerations without explaining the biology-STS connection.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Mitosis produces daughter cells that are
answer: genetically identical to the parent cell

choices.text: genetically identical to the parent cell
haploid with half the chromosome number always
genetically different due to crossing over
lacking any nuclear material

explanation: Mitosis maintains chromosome number and genetic identity for growth and repair.
common_mistake: Crossing over occurs in meiosis, not mitosis.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The stage of mitosis during which chromosomes align at the cell equator is
answer: metaphase

choices.text: metaphase
prophase
anaphase
telophase

explanation: Metaphase is characterized by chromosomes aligned on the metaphase plate.
common_mistake: Anaphase involves sister chromatid separation.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Sister chromatids separate during
answer: anaphase of mitosis

choices.text: anaphase of mitosis
prophase of meiosis I only
interphase before DNA replication
cytokinesis exclusively

explanation: Anaphase involves cohesin breakdown and movement of sister chromatids to opposite poles.
common_mistake: Homologous chromosomes separate in meiosis I; sisters in meiosis II and mitosis anaphase.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Cytokinesis in animal cells occurs by
answer: formation of a cleavage furrow

choices.text: formation of a cleavage furrow
building a cell plate from the centre outward
dissolving the nuclear envelope permanently without division
meiosis without DNA replication

explanation: Animal cells pinch inward via actin-myosin ring. Plants form a cell plate.
common_mistake: Cell plates are characteristic of plant cytokinesis.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Locating DNA replication in cell cycle
question_text: The cell cycle phase when DNA replication occurs is
answer: S phase of interphase

choices.text: S phase of interphase
G1 before any synthesis
M phase during mitosis
G0 permanently in all cells

explanation: DNA is duplicated during S (synthesis) phase before mitosis.
common_mistake: Replication does not occur during M phase.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Explaining reduction division in meiosis I
question_text: Meiosis I is described as reduction division because it
answer: separates homologous chromosome pairs producing haploid cells

choices.text: separates homologous chromosome pairs producing haploid cells
duplicates DNA without cell division
produces four diploid cells
only occurs in somatic skin cells

explanation: Meiosis I halves the chromosome number by separating homologues.
common_mistake: Meiosis produces haploid, not diploid, products.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Crossing over during prophase I of meiosis contributes to
answer: genetic recombination between homologous chromosomes

choices.text: genetic recombination between homologous chromosomes
identical daughter cells in every division
doubling of chromosome number in gametes
elimination of all allelic variation

explanation: Exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids creates new allele combinations.
common_mistake: Mitosis does not involve crossing over.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Independent assortment of chromosomes occurs during
answer: metaphase I of meiosis

choices.text: metaphase I of meiosis
interphase of mitosis only
cytokinesis of meiosis II exclusively
DNA replication in S phase

explanation: Random alignment of homologous pairs at metaphase I leads to independent assortment.
common_mistake: Sister chromatid separation is not independent assortment.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A cell with diploid number $2n = 12$ after meiosis II produces gametes with
answer: $n = 6$ chromosomes each

choices.text: $n = 6$ chromosomes each
$n = 12$ chromosomes each
$n = 3$ chromosomes each
$n = 24$ chromosomes each

explanation: Meiosis halves the diploid number: $12 \div 2 = 6$.
common_mistake: Students forget meiosis halves chromosome number.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Synapsis and formation of tetrads occur in
answer: prophase I of meiosis

choices.text: prophase I of meiosis
prophase of mitosis
anaphase II of meiosis
G2 of interphase

explanation: Homologous chromosomes pair as tetrads during meiotic prophase I.
common_mistake: Mitotic prophase has no synapsis.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Predicting nondisjunction consequences in meiosis I
question_text: Nondisjunction in meiosis I results in gametes with
answer: an extra or missing homologous chromosome

choices.text: an extra or missing homologous chromosome
always perfect haploid sets
only mitochondrial DNA abnormalities
no effect on offspring chromosome number

explanation: Failure of homologue separation yields n+1 and n-1 gametes.
common_mistake: Nondisjunction has significant effects on zygote karyotype.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) most commonly arises from
answer: nondisjunction during maternal meiosis I

choices.text: nondisjunction during maternal meiosis I
crossing over in sperm mitosis
DNA replication in G1 phase
cytokinesis failure in zygote mitosis only

explanation: Trisomy 21 typically results from meiotic nondisjunction producing n+1 gamete.
common_mistake: Trisomy involves meiotic error, not mitotic crossing over.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A karyotype showing 47, XXY indicates
answer: Klinefelter syndrome

choices.text: Klinefelter syndrome
Turner syndrome (45,X)
normal male 46,XY
trisomy 21

explanation: XXY is Klinefelter. Turner is 45,X; normal male is 46,XY.
common_mistake: Sex chromosome aneuploidies are commonly confused.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Turner syndrome karyotype is typically

choices.text: 47, XXY
46, XX
47, XXX

explanation: Monosomy X (45,X) causes Turner syndrome in females.
common_mistake: XXY is Klinefelter; XXX is trisomy X.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The homologous region of X and Y chromosomes is important during
answer: meiosis because X and Y can pair and segregate

choices.text: meiosis because X and Y can pair and segregate
mitosis because homologues always pair in somatic cells
cytokinesis in bacteria
transcription in prokaryotes without a nucleus

explanation: Pseudoautosomal regions allow X-Y pairing in meiosis I.
common_mistake: Somatic mitosis does not involve homologue pairing.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Chiasmata visible during meiosis represent
answer: sites where crossing over occurred between homologues

choices.text: sites where crossing over occurred between homologues
centromeres attaching to spindle fibres in mitosis only
nuclear pores for RNA export
replication origins in interphase only

explanation: Chiasmata are physical manifestations of crossovers holding homologues together.
common_mistake: Chiasmata are meiosis-specific crossover sites.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Comparing DNA content across cell cycle phases
question_text: A somatic cell in G2 phase of the cell cycle contains
answer: twice the DNA content of the same cell in G1

choices.text: twice the DNA content of the same cell in G1
half the DNA of G1 due to division
no DNA because replication has not started
four times the DNA of a gamete at all times

explanation: After S phase, DNA content doubles until mitosis divides it.
common_mistake: G2 follows replication; DNA is doubled relative to G1.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Relating cell cycle regulation to cancer
question_text: Cancer cells often exhibit uncontrolled division partly because of
answer: mutations in genes regulating the cell cycle checkpoints

choices.text: mutations in genes regulating the cell cycle checkpoints
complete loss of all DNA
inability to replicate DNA ever
permanent arrest in G0 in all cases

explanation: Oncogene activation or tumour suppressor loss disrupts checkpoints (e.g., p53).
common_mistake: Cancer involves excessive, not absent, division.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The mitotic index of a tissue sample measures
answer: the proportion of cells undergoing mitosis

choices.text: the proportion of cells undergoing mitosis
the total number of chromosomes in the organism
the rate of crossing over in gametes
the concentration of hormones in blood

explanation: Mitotic index = (cells in mitosis / total cells) × 100%, indicating proliferation rate.
common_mistake: Mitotic index is about division frequency, not chromosome count.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Colchicine disrupts spindle formation. Treating a dividing cell with colchicine during metaphase would most likely
answer: prevent chromosome movement to poles, arresting division

choices.text: prevent chromosome movement to poles, arresting division
accelerate sister chromatid separation
cause immediate completion of cytokinesis
induce meiosis in somatic cells

explanation: Without functional spindles, chromosomes cannot segregate properly.
common_mistake: Spindle disruption blocks movement, not accelerates it.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Order mitosis phases: (1) anaphase, (2) metaphase, (3) prophase, (4) telophase. Record all four digits.
explanation: Prophase (3), metaphase (2), anaphase (1), telophase (4).
common_mistake: Anaphase placed before metaphase.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Order meiosis I stages: (1) telophase I, (2) metaphase I, (3) prophase I, (4) anaphase I. Record all four digits.
explanation: Prophase I (3), metaphase I (2), anaphase I (4), telophase I (1).
common_mistake: Meiosis I anaphase confused with mitosis timing.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Calculating chromosome number after meiosis I
question_text: A cell has $2n = 16$. How many chromosomes are in each cell immediately after meiosis I? Record the two-digit answer.
explanation: Meiosis I halves the number: $16 \div 2 = 8$ chromosomes per cell (still duplicated chromatids).
common_mistake: Students answer 16 or 4 incorrectly.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Compare mitosis and meiosis in terms of purpose, number of divisions, and genetic variation in daughter cells.
answer: Mitosis: one division producing two genetically identical diploid cells for growth/repair; no crossing over. Meiosis: two divisions producing four genetically unique haploid gametes; crossing over and independent assortment generate variation.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Purpose contrast — 2 marks. (2) Division number — 1 mark. (3) Ploidy of products — 2 marks. (4) Variation mechanisms in meiosis — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students state meiosis produces identical cells or omit variation sources.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Explain how nondisjunction during meiosis can lead to trisomy in a zygote. Include which division may be affected and the chromosome number in the resulting gamete.
answer: If homologues fail to separate in meiosis I, or sister chromatids in meiosis II, a gamete receives n+1 chromosomes. When fertilized by a normal n gamete, the zygote is 2n+1 (trisomy). Example: n+1 gamete with chromosome 21 plus normal gamete yields trisomy 21 (47 chromosomes).
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Define nondisjunction — 1 mark. (2) Identify meiosis I or II — 2 marks. (3) n+1 gamete described — 2 marks. (4) Zygote trisomy outcome — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students describe trisomy without explaining gamete chromosome number.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: During prophase of mitosis, each chromosome consists of
answer: two sister chromatids joined at a centromere

choices.text: two sister chromatids joined at a centromere
four homologous chromatids in a tetrad
a single unduplicated chromatid always
only circular DNA without proteins

explanation: DNA replication in S phase creates sister chromatids visible in mitotic prophase.
common_mistake: Tetrads are meiosis I structures.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The centromere functions to
answer: attach chromatids to spindle fibres via kinetochores

choices.text: attach chromatids to spindle fibres via kinetochores
replicate DNA during S phase directly
produce ribosomes for the cell
synthesize all cellular lipids

explanation: Kinetochores at centromeres connect chromatids to spindle microtubules.
common_mistake: DNA polymerase replicates DNA, not centromeres.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Binary fission in bacteria differs from eukaryotic mitosis because binary fission
answer: does not involve a mitotic spindle or nuclear envelope breakdown

choices.text: does not involve a mitotic spindle or nuclear envelope breakdown
always produces four haploid cells
requires crossing over between homologues
only occurs in human liver cells

explanation: Prokaryotes lack spindle apparatus and membrane-bound nucleus.
common_mistake: Bacterial division is not meiotic.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A cell in metaphase of mitosis has a chromosome count of
answer: 2n with each chromosome having two chromatids

choices.text: 2n with each chromosome having two chromatids
n haploid chromosomes only
4n due to two rounds of replication without division
zero chromosomes visible

explanation: Metaphase mitotic cells are diploid (2n) with duplicated chromosomes.
common_mistake: n chromosomes describe gametes or post-meiotic cells.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Polyploidy in plants can result from
answer: failure of cytokinesis after mitosis or meiosis

choices.text: failure of cytokinesis after mitosis or meiosis
only bacterial conjugation
removal of all centromeres
exclusive male meiosis without females

explanation: Cytokinesis failure doubles chromosome sets, common in plant breeding.
common_mistake: Polyploidy is not caused by bacterial conjugation in plants.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Describing SRY gene role in sex determination
question_text: The SRY gene on the Y chromosome triggers
answer: development of testes in mammalian embryogenesis

choices.text: development of testes in mammalian embryogenesis
ovarian development regardless of other genes
mitosis in bacterial cells
photosynthesis in leaf cells

explanation: SRY initiates male gonadal differentiation pathway.
common_mistake: Ovarian default pathway occurs without SRY function.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Applying sex determination to ZW system
question_text: In birds, females are ZW and males are ZZ. A female bird produces gametes with
answer: either Z or W chromosome (one sex chromosome each)

choices.text: either Z or W chromosome (one sex chromosome each)
always ZZ like males
no sex chromosomes in any egg
four W chromosomes per egg

explanation: Female birds (ZW) produce Z or W gametes by meiotic segregation.
common_mistake: ZZ is the male bird karyotype.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Interphase is sometimes called the resting phase, but it is metabolically active because
answer: the cell grows and replicates DNA in preparation for division

choices.text: the cell grows and replicates DNA in preparation for division
no biochemical activity occurs at all
chromosomes are visible and moving to poles
crossing over occurs between tetrads

explanation: Interphase includes G1, S, and G2 with high metabolic activity.
common_mistake: Chromosome movement occurs in M phase, not interphase.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A biopsy shows 8% mitotic index versus 2% in normal tissue. This suggests
answer: higher cell proliferation rate in the biopsy tissue

choices.text: higher cell proliferation rate in the biopsy tissue
complete absence of cell division
all cells are in G0 permanently
meiosis occurring in somatic liver cells

explanation: Elevated mitotic index indicates more cells dividing per unit time.
common_mistake: Higher, not lower, division is indicated.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Checkpoint proteins such as p53 can halt the cell cycle if
answer: DNA damage is detected, allowing repair before division

choices.text: DNA damage is detected, allowing repair before division
the cell has perfect DNA always skipping G1
spindle fibres form too early in G1
all hormones are absent from blood

explanation: p53 activates repair pathways or apoptosis when damage is sensed.
common_mistake: Checkpoints respond to damage, not hormone absence alone.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Telophase of mitosis is characterized by
answer: reformation of nuclear envelopes around daughter nuclei

choices.text: reformation of nuclear envelopes around daughter nuclei
alignment of chromosomes at the equator
separation of homologous chromosomes
crossing over between non-sister chromatids

explanation: Telophase reverses prophase events: nuclei reform, chromosomes decondense.
common_mistake: Equatorial alignment is metaphase.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Meiosis II is most similar to mitosis because it
answer: separates sister chromatids in each haploid cell

choices.text: separates sister chromatids in each haploid cell
pairs homologous chromosomes in tetrads
duplicates DNA before starting
produces diploid daughter cells

explanation: Meiosis II aligns and separates sister chromatids like mitosis.
common_mistake: Homologue pairing is meiosis I only.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A primary oocyte is arrested in prophase I until
answer: puberty when hormonal signals resume meiosis monthly

choices.text: puberty when hormonal signals resume meiosis monthly
birth when it immediately completes meiosis IV
fertilization of a sperm in the testes
implantation in the seminiferous tubule

explanation: Oogenesis arrests at prophase I for years until ovulation cycle resumes.
common_mistake: Meiosis IV does not exist.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Spermatogenesis produces functional sperm beginning at
answer: puberty and continuing throughout adult life

choices.text: puberty and continuing throughout adult life
before birth only in the ovary
after menopause in females
only once during embryonic development

explanation: Males continuously produce sperm from puberty onward.
common_mistake: Female gametogenesis timing differs from male.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Calculating diploid number from chromatid count
question_text: A human cell with 92 chromatids during mitotic metaphase has diploid number
explanation: 92 chromatids = 46 duplicated chromosomes, so 2n = 46.
common_mistake: Chromatid count is twice chromosome count at metaphase.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: If mitotic index is $6$% and $500$ cells are counted, how many cells are in mitosis? Record the two-digit answer.
explanation: $500 \times 0.06 = 30$ cells.
common_mistake: Students multiply by 6 instead of 0.06.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A species has $2n = 20$. How many chromosomes per gamete? Record the two-digit answer.
common_mistake: Students give 20 or 5.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Describe the events of prophase I of meiosis that contribute to genetic variation, naming at least two processes.
answer: Synapsis forms tetrads allowing crossing over between non-sister chromatids of homologues, creating recombinant chromosomes. Independent assortment at metaphase I randomizes which homologue orientations segregate to each pole, producing diverse gamete combinations.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Synapsis/tetrad — 1 mark. (2) Crossing over — 2 marks. (3) Independent assortment — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students mention only one variation source.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Mendel's law of segregation states that
answer: two alleles for a trait separate during gamete formation

choices.text: two alleles for a trait separate during gamete formation
all genes assort independently regardless of chromosome location
dominant alleles physically blend with recessive alleles
only sex chromosomes segregate during meiosis

explanation: Each gamete receives one allele of each gene as homologues separate in meiosis I.
common_mistake: Independent assortment is a separate law from segregation.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A heterozygous individual (Aa) exhibits the dominant phenotype because
answer: one dominant allele is sufficient to express the trait

choices.text: one dominant allele is sufficient to express the trait
the recessive allele is always deleted from DNA
dominant alleles code for no functional protein
both alleles are always equally expressed in all cells

explanation: Complete dominance: one functional dominant allele masks recessive in heterozygotes.
common_mistake: Recessive alleles remain present but may not affect phenotype.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A testcross (Aa × aa) is used to
answer: determine whether an individual with dominant phenotype is homozygous or heterozygous

choices.text: determine whether an individual with dominant phenotype is homozygous or heterozygous
always produce only homozygous dominant offspring
map genes on the Y chromosome exclusively
prevent crossing over during meiosis

explanation: Crossing with homozygous recessive reveals hidden recessive alleles in offspring ratios.
common_mistake: Testcrosses reveal genotype, not prevent crossing over.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: In a dihybrid cross AaBb × AaBb with genes on different chromosomes, the expected phenotypic ratio is

choices.text: 3:1 only
1:1:1:1 always for every dihybrid
1:2:1 for both traits combined

explanation: Independent assortment of two heterozygous genes yields classic 9:3:3:1 ratio.
common_mistake: 3:1 is monohybrid ratio, not dihybrid.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Predicting linkage effects on offspring ratios
question_text: Linked genes on the same chromosome violate independent assortment and produce
answer: more parental-type offspring than recombinant types in test crosses

choices.text: more parental-type offspring than recombinant types in test crosses
exactly 9:3:3:1 ratios always
no offspring ever
only homozygous offspring

explanation: Linkage reduces recombinant frequency; parental combinations predominate unless crossing over separates alleles.
common_mistake: 9:3:3:1 assumes unlinked genes.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Haemophilia is X-linked recessive. A carrier mother (X$^H$X$^h$) and unaffected father (X$^H$Y) have sons with haemophilia with probability

choices.text: 0% because fathers pass X to sons
100% for all children
25% for daughters only

explanation: Sons inherit X from mother: half receive X$^h$ and are affected.
common_mistake: Fathers pass Y to sons, not X; mother's X determines son phenotype.
