unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Predicting X-linked carrier status in offspring
question_text: Red-green colour blindness is X-linked recessive. An affected male and unaffected female (no family history) have daughters who are
answer: all carriers (phenotypically normal if mother homozygous dominant)

choices.text: all carriers (phenotypically normal if mother homozygous dominant)
all affected because daughters get father's Y
never carriers because X-linked traits skip females
homozygous recessive always

explanation: Affected father passes X to all daughters; if mother is homozygous dominant, daughters are carriers.
common_mistake: Fathers pass X to daughters, not Y.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Watson and Crick's DNA model proposed that the two strands are
answer: antiparallel and held together by complementary base pairing

choices.text: antiparallel and held together by complementary base pairing
parallel with identical nucleotide sequences
connected by peptide bonds between bases
single-stranded with no hydrogen bonds

explanation: Antiparallel 5' to 3' orientation with A-T and G-C hydrogen bonding defines the double helix.
common_mistake: Peptide bonds join amino acids, not DNA strands.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Chargaff's rules state that in DNA
answer: the amount of adenine equals thymine and guanine equals cytosine

choices.text: the amount of adenine equals thymine and guanine equals cytosine
adenine equals guanine in all species
thymine equals cytosine always
all four bases are present in equal proportions

explanation: Complementary base pairing explains Chargaff's equality: A=T, G=C.
common_mistake: A pairs with T, not G.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Semiconservative DNA replication means each daughter molecule contains
answer: one original strand and one newly synthesized strand

choices.text: one original strand and one newly synthesized strand
two entirely new strands with no original template
only RNA without any DNA
four identical strands in one helix

explanation: Meselson-Stahl demonstrated each new DNA helix retains one parental strand.
common_mistake: Conservative model (all old strands together) was disproved.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Explaining role of primase and polymerase
question_text: DNA polymerase III in prokaryotes requires a primer because it
answer: can only add nucleotides to an existing 3' OH group

choices.text: can only add nucleotides to an existing 3' OH group
synthesizes DNA in the 3' to 5' direction exclusively
does not need template DNA
replaces RNA with amino acids directly

explanation: Primase provides short RNA primer with 3' OH for polymerase elongation in 5'→3' direction.
common_mistake: DNA polymerase extends 5'→3', not 3'→5'.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Distinguishing leading and lagging strand synthesis
question_text: The leading strand during replication is synthesized
answer: continuously toward the replication fork

choices.text: continuously toward the replication fork
only in short Okazaki fragments away from the fork
without any template
only after all RNA is removed from ribosomes

explanation: Leading strand synthesis proceeds continuously 5'→3' as fork opens.
common_mistake: Lagging strand uses Okazaki fragments.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Okazaki fragments are found on the
answer: lagging strand and are later joined by DNA ligase

choices.text: lagging strand and are later joined by DNA ligase
leading strand only without any joining
mRNA during transcription exclusively
protein primary structure during translation

explanation: Discontinuous synthesis on lagging strand produces Okazaki fragments sealed by ligase.
common_mistake: Leading strand is continuous, not fragmented.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: During transcription, RNA polymerase binds to the
answer: promoter region upstream of the gene

choices.text: promoter region upstream of the gene
tRNA anticodon loop
ribosome P site exclusively
centromere during anaphase

explanation: Promoters (e.g., TATA box in eukaryotes) initiate transcription.
common_mistake: Anticodons are on tRNA, not promoter DNA.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes splicing to
answer: remove introns and join exons before export from nucleus

choices.text: remove introns and join exons before export from nucleus
add amino acids to growing polypeptide chains
replicate DNA semiconservatively
produce Okazaki fragments on lagging strand

explanation: Spliceosome removes introns; mature mRNA contains continuous exon sequence.
common_mistake: Splicing is RNA processing, not translation.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The genetic code is degenerate because
answer: most amino acids are encoded by more than one codon

choices.text: most amino acids are encoded by more than one codon
each codon specifies multiple different amino acids
DNA has no thymine in any organism
all proteins have identical primary sequences

explanation: Degeneracy: 64 codons code for 20 amino acids with redundancy (e.g., leucine has six codons).
common_mistake: Each codon specifies one amino acid (or stop); degeneracy refers to multiple codons per amino acid.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A nonsense mutation introduces a
answer: premature stop codon in the mRNA

choices.text: premature stop codon in the mRNA
change in regulatory DNA far from any gene only
duplication of an entire chromosome always
new start codon at the beginning of every intron

explanation: Nonsense mutations create early stop codons, truncating the protein.
common_mistake: Missense changes one amino acid; nonsense creates stop.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A frameshift mutation caused by insertion of one nucleotide will
answer: alter every amino acid downstream of the insertion site

choices.text: alter every amino acid downstream of the insertion site
change only one amino acid without affecting downstream sequence
always improve protein function
prevent transcription from occurring entirely

explanation: Reading frame shift changes codon grouping for all subsequent sequence.
common_mistake: Single nucleotide insertion is not a silent point mutation.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: During translation, the ribosome reads mRNA codons and matches them to
answer: anticodons on tRNA carrying specific amino acids

choices.text: anticodons on tRNA carrying specific amino acids
DNA bases on the lagging strand
histone proteins in nucleosomes only
promoter sequences on chromosome 1 only

explanation: tRNA anticodon pairs with mRNA codon; amino acid is added to polypeptide chain.
common_mistake: Translation uses RNA, not DNA template directly.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The start codon AUG codes for
answer: methionine and initiates translation

choices.text: methionine and initiates translation
phenylalanine only
a stop signal ending the protein
thymine in the DNA strand

explanation: AUG is both methionine codon and universal translation start in eukaryotes.
common_mistake: UAA, UAG, UGA are stop codons, not AUG.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) amplifies DNA by using
answer: cycles of denaturation, primer annealing, and extension with thermostable polymerase

choices.text: cycles of denaturation, primer annealing, and extension with thermostable polymerase
meiosis and fertilization in a test tube
only RNA polymerase without any primers
crossing over between homologous chromosomes in vitro

explanation: PCR thermocycles separate strands, bind primers, and extend with Taq polymerase.
common_mistake: PCR is amplification, not meiosis.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments primarily by
answer: size, with smaller fragments migrating farther through the gel

choices.text: size, with smaller fragments migrating farther through the gel
colour of nitrogenous bases exclusively
the age of the DNA sample
number of chromosomes in the organism only

explanation: Negatively charged DNA moves toward positive electrode; smaller pieces travel faster.
common_mistake: Base colour does not determine gel migration distance.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing uses guide RNA to
answer: direct Cas9 nuclease to a specific DNA sequence for cleavage

choices.text: direct Cas9 nuclease to a specific DNA sequence for cleavage
translate mRNA into protein without ribosomes
replicate entire chromosomes without polymerase
prevent all mutations from ever occurring in nature

explanation: Guide RNA base-pairs with target DNA; Cas9 creates double-strand break for repair/editing.
common_mistake: CRISPR edits DNA; it does not perform translation.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited primarily from the
answer: mother because sperm contribute few mitochondria to the zygote

choices.text: mother because sperm contribute few mitochondria to the zygote
father because Y chromosome carries all mitochondria
placenta exclusively without any cellular DNA
environment with no genetic component

explanation: Maternal cytoplasm donates mitochondria; paternal contribution is minimal and often degraded.
common_mistake: Y chromosome does not contain mitochondrial genome.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A pedigree showing an affected father and affected daughter but unaffected mother suggests the trait is most likely
answer: autosomal dominant

choices.text: autosomal dominant
X-linked recessive only with no exceptions
Y-linked recessive affecting daughters
mitochondrial with paternal transmission

explanation: Father-to-daughter transmission with affected father rules out X-linked recessive (father passes Y to sons). Autosomal dominant fits.
common_mistake: Y-linked traits affect males only; daughters cannot inherit Y-linked disease genes from father.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: In a monohybrid cross Tt × Tt, what fraction of offspring are expected to be homozygous recessive? Express as a decimal rounded to two decimal places.
explanation: Punnett square yields 1/4 tt = 0.25.
common_mistake: Students answer 0.50 confusing heterozygous fraction.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Order protein synthesis steps: (1) tRNA delivers amino acids, (2) mRNA exits nucleus, (3) transcription, (4) ribosome assembly on mRNA. Record all four digits.
explanation: Transcription (3), mRNA processing/export (2), ribosome binds mRNA (4), tRNA delivers amino acids (1).
common_mistake: Translation steps placed before transcription.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Describing central dogma and gene expression
question_text: Explain the central dogma of molecular biology and describe what happens during each of transcription and translation in a eukaryotic cell.
answer: Central dogma: DNA → RNA → protein. Transcription: RNA polymerase synthesizes mRNA using DNA template in nucleus; pre-mRNA is processed (capping, splicing, poly-A tail). Translation: mature mRNA exported to cytoplasm; ribosome reads codons; tRNA brings amino acids; polypeptide elongates until stop codon.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Central dogma stated — 1 mark. (2) Transcription details — 3 marks. (3) Translation details — 3 marks.
common_mistake: Students omit nuclear vs cytoplasmic compartmentalization in eukaryotes.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A student claims a trait showing affected males passing to all daughters but no sons is X-linked dominant. Evaluate this claim using pedigree reasoning.
answer: Affected males pass X to daughters only (not sons). If all daughters of affected fathers are affected, the allele on father's X is expressed in daughters — consistent with X-linked dominant. Sons inherit father's Y, so they would not receive father's X-linked allele. The claim is reasonable if mothers are unaffected or heterozygous; if sons of carrier mothers can be affected, X-linked pattern must be distinguished from autosomal.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Father transmits X to daughters — 2 marks. (2) Sons inherit Y from father — 1 mark. (3) Evaluate consistency with X-linked dominant — 2 marks. (4) Note alternative patterns if needed — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students fail to note father-son transmission pattern for X-linked traits.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Codominance in AB blood type means
answer: both IA and IB alleles are fully expressed in heterozygotes

choices.text: both IA and IB alleles are fully expressed in heterozygotes
only the recessive i allele is expressed
the phenotype is always intermediate like pink flowers
neither allele is present in red blood cells

explanation: AB individuals express both A and B antigens — codominance.
common_mistake: Incomplete dominance blends phenotypes; codominance shows both.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Incomplete dominance in snapdragon flower colour (red × white → pink) demonstrates
answer: heterozygotes with an intermediate phenotype

choices.text: heterozygotes with an intermediate phenotype
complete masking of recessive allele
X-linked recessive inheritance
linkage on the Y chromosome

explanation: Rr heterozygotes are pink — blended intermediate expression.
common_mistake: Pink is intermediate, not dominant masking.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A Punnett square for Aa × Aa shows genotypic ratio
answer: 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa

choices.text: 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa
1:1 Aa:aa only
all AA

explanation: Monohybrid self-cross genotypic ratio is 1:2:1.
common_mistake: 9:3:3:1 is dihybrid phenotypic ratio.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Blood type parent with genotype IAi × IBi can produce offspring with types
answer: A, B, AB, and O

choices.text: A, B, AB, and O
only AB
only O
A and B but never AB or O

explanation: Cross can yield IAIB (AB), IAi (A), IBi (B), ii (O).
common_mistake: All four blood types are possible from this cross.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Huntington disease is autosomal dominant. An affected heterozygous parent (Hh) × unaffected (hh) has affected offspring probability

choices.text: 25% only for females

explanation: Hh × hh gives 50% Hh affected, 50% hh unaffected.
common_mistake: Dominant heterozygote transmits H to half of offspring.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Cystic fibrosis is autosomal recessive. Two carrier parents (Cc × Cc) have affected child probability
explanation: Cc × Cc yields 1/4 cc affected.
common_mistake: Carrier × carrier gives 25% affected, 50% carrier.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Calculating base composition from Chargaff rules
question_text: A double helix has 30% adenine. What percent is guanine?
explanation: A=T=30%. Remaining 40% is G+C; equal G and C gives 20% each.
common_mistake: Students forget Chargaff: G=C and A+G+T+C=100%.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The 5' cap on eukaryotic mRNA functions to
answer: protect mRNA and assist ribosome binding during translation initiation

choices.text: protect mRNA and assist ribosome binding during translation initiation
terminate transcription at the promoter
join Okazaki fragments
catalyze peptide bond formation directly

explanation: 5' cap stabilizes mRNA and is recognized by initiation factors.
common_mistake: Cap is added after transcription starts, not at termination.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: The poly-A tail on mRNA
answer: increases stability and assists nuclear export

choices.text: increases stability and assists nuclear export
codes for lysine amino acids
replaces all introns during splicing
directs DNA replication origin firing

explanation: Polyadenylation protects 3' end from degradation.
common_mistake: Poly-A is not translated into amino acids.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A silent (synonymous) point mutation
answer: may not change the amino acid due to code degeneracy

choices.text: may not change the amino acid due to code degeneracy
always changes protein folding catastrophically
deletes an entire chromosome
prevents RNA polymerase from binding always

explanation: Third-base wobble often allows same amino acid despite base change.
common_mistake: Silent mutations can still affect splicing but often preserve amino acid.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A missense mutation
answer: substitutes one amino acid for another in the polypeptide

choices.text: substitutes one amino acid for another in the polypeptide
introduces a premature stop codon always
has no effect on protein sequence ever
duplicates an entire gene cluster

explanation: Missense changes one codon to code different amino acid.
common_mistake: Nonsense creates stop; missense changes amino acid.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Reverse transcriptase in retroviruses synthesizes
answer: DNA from an RNA template

choices.text: DNA from an RNA template
RNA from protein template
lipids from carbohydrate template
DNA without any template molecule

explanation: Retroviruses convert viral RNA genome to DNA for host integration.
common_mistake: Normal transcription is DNA→RNA, not RNA→DNA.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: DNA fingerprinting using STR analysis compares
answer: variable number tandem repeats between individuals

choices.text: variable number tandem repeats between individuals
only mitochondrial ribosomes
the entire genome sequence of every cell identically
amino acid sequences of all haemoglobin molecules only

explanation: STR alleles differ in repeat count, providing individual-specific profiles.
common_mistake: STRs are non-coding repetitive DNA markers.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A restriction enzyme cuts DNA at
answer: specific recognition sequences producing defined fragments

choices.text: specific recognition sequences producing defined fragments
random locations without any sequence specificity
only RNA polymerase binding sites during transcription
centromeres during every mitosis automatically

explanation: Restriction endonucleases recognize palindromic sequences.
common_mistake: Specificity is key to molecular cloning.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A recombinant plasmid contains
answer: foreign DNA inserted into a bacterial plasmid vector

choices.text: foreign DNA inserted into a bacterial plasmid vector
only viral RNA without DNA
human ribosomes for translation in nucleus
meiotic spindles for chromosome separation

explanation: Vectors carry inserted genes into host bacteria for replication/expression.
common_mistake: Plasmids are DNA vectors, not ribosome structures.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Gene therapy aims to
answer: introduce functional genes to compensate for defective alleles

choices.text: introduce functional genes to compensate for defective alleles
eliminate all allelic variation in populations
prevent meiosis in somatic cells
replace mitosis with binary fission in humans

explanation: Therapeutic genes are delivered via vectors to restore function.
common_mistake: Gene therapy targets defective genes, not meiosis.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Epigenetic methylation of DNA can
answer: silence gene expression without changing nucleotide sequence

choices.text: silence gene expression without changing nucleotide sequence
change the primary DNA sequence permanently
replace transcription with DNA replication
cause immediate frameshift in all genes

explanation: Methyl groups on cytosine often repress transcription.
common_mistake: Epigenetics modifies expression, not primary sequence.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A lac operon in bacteria is induced when
answer: lactose is present, relieving repression of transcription

choices.text: lactose is present, relieving repression of transcription
glucose binds the operator directly always
RNA polymerase is permanently absent from bacteria
all genes are always transcribed at maximum rate

explanation: Lactose/allolactose inactivates repressor, allowing lac gene transcription.
common_mistake: Glucose preference involves catabolite repression, separate mechanism.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A nonsense suppressor tRNA might
answer: read through a stop codon inserting an amino acid instead of terminating

choices.text: read through a stop codon inserting an amino acid instead of terminating
delete the entire chromosome 21
prevent all translation from ever starting
convert DNA into protein without RNA

explanation: Suppressor tRNAs mutate anticodon to pair with stop codon, extending chain.
common_mistake: Suppressors affect termination, not DNA structure.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Sickle cell anemia involves a point mutation in the beta-globin gene changing
answer: glutamic acid to valine at position 6

choices.text: glutamic acid to valine at position 6
methionine to stop codon at position 1 always
entire deletion of chromosome 11
conversion of haemoglobin to insulin

explanation: GAG→GTG substitutes valine, creating HbS with altered shape.
common_mistake: Single amino acid change causes sickling under low O2.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Relating tumour suppressor mutations to cancer
question_text: BRCA1 mutations increase cancer risk because they impair
answer: DNA repair pathways for double-strand breaks

choices.text: DNA repair pathways for double-strand breaks
all mitosis in neurons exclusively
digestion of proteins in the stomach
secretion of insulin from beta cells

explanation: BRCA1 participates in homologous recombination repair; loss increases mutation accumulation.
common_mistake: BRCA1 is DNA repair, not digestive function.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A karyotype of a cancer cell might show aneuploidy because
answer: checkpoint failures allow division with incorrect chromosome segregation

choices.text: checkpoint failures allow division with incorrect chromosome segregation
meiosis occurs in all liver cells normally
DNA never replicates in cancer cells
crossing over prevents all division

explanation: Cancer often exhibits chromosomal instability from checkpoint defects.
common_mistake: Cancer involves mitotic errors, not routine meiosis in somatic cells.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Homology among genes in different species suggests
answer: shared evolutionary ancestry

choices.text: shared evolutionary ancestry
the genes have no functional relationship ever
convergent evolution with no common origin always
identical chromosomal location on Y in all species

explanation: Sequence similarity reflects descent from common ancestral gene.
common_mistake: Homology indicates common ancestry, not random similarity.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A phylogenetic tree based on mitochondrial DNA sequences helps determine
answer: evolutionary relationships and maternal lineage patterns

choices.text: evolutionary relationships and maternal lineage patterns
only the blood type of living individuals
the rate of photosynthesis in plants exclusively
daily hormone cycles in teenagers

explanation: mtDNA evolves at measurable rate useful for phylogeny and maternal line tracing.
common_mistake: mtDNA is not used for ABO blood typing.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A DNA sample is 22% guanine. What percent is adenine? Record the two-digit answer.
common_mistake: Students subtract 22 from 100 without halving.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Cross AaBb × aabb. What fraction of offspring show both dominant traits? Express as decimal to two places.
explanation: Each gene segregates independently: 1/2 dominant for each → 1/4 both dominant.
common_mistake: Students add probabilities instead of multiplying.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: How many possible codons exist in the genetic code? Record the two-digit answer.
explanation: Each of three positions has 4 options: 4×4×4=64 codons.
common_mistake: Students answer 20 (number of amino acids) or 61.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Describe how a deletion of two base pairs in the middle of an open reading frame can affect the resulting protein.
answer: Deleting two bases shifts the reading frame, altering every subsequent codon. A premature stop codon may appear, producing a truncated nonfunctional protein. If translation continues, the amino acid sequence is entirely wrong downstream, typically eliminating normal function.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Frameshift described — 2 marks. (2) Downstream codon changes — 2 marks. (3) Functional consequence — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students claim only one amino acid changes.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Explain how gel electrophoresis and PCR could be used together to confirm whether two individuals share a specific DNA marker.
answer: PCR amplifies the target region using specific primers. Gel electrophoresis separates products by size. Matching band positions indicate identical allele sizes; different migration indicates different alleles.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) PCR amplification — 2 marks. (2) Gel separation — 2 marks. (3) Band comparison — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students describe techniques without connecting them.

question_text: A population is best defined as
answer: all individuals of the same species living in a defined area at a given time

choices.text: all individuals of the same species living in a defined area at a given time
all organisms of every species in a biome globally
only the dominant predators in an ecosystem
the physical habitat without any living organisms

explanation: Population ecology focuses on one species within a specific geographic area.
common_mistake: Community includes multiple species; population is single species.

question_text: The gene pool of a population refers to
answer: the total collection of alleles present in all individuals

choices.text: the total collection of alleles present in all individuals
only dominant alleles that are expressed phenotypically
the mitochondria inherited from fathers
the food web connecting producers and consumers

explanation: Gene pool encompasses every allele frequency for all gene loci in the population.
common_mistake: Recessive alleles are part of the gene pool even when not expressed.

question_text: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium assumes
answer: no mutation, no migration, large population, random mating, and no natural selection

choices.text: no mutation, no migration, large population, random mating, and no natural selection
constant natural selection favouring recessive alleles always
small population size with frequent genetic drift only
non-random mating increasing homozygosity always

explanation: Five conditions (no evolution factors) must hold for allele frequencies to remain constant generation to generation.
common_mistake: Natural selection violates Hardy-Weinberg assumptions.

question_text: In Hardy-Weinberg, if $p$ is the frequency of allele A and $q$ of allele a, the expected frequency of genotype Aa is
answer: $2pq$

choices.text: $2pq$
$pq$

explanation: Heterozygote frequency under HW equilibrium is $2pq$.
common_mistake: $p^2$ is AA homozygous; $q^2$ is aa homozygous.

question_text: Genetic drift has the strongest effect when
answer: population size is small

choices.text: population size is small
population size is infinitely large
no alleles exist in the gene pool
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium is perfectly met

explanation: Random sampling error in allele transmission disproportionately affects small populations.
common_mistake: Large populations buffer against drift.

question_text: The founder effect is a form of
answer: genetic drift when a small group establishes a new population

choices.text: genetic drift when a small group establishes a new population
directional selection increasing fitness always
competitive exclusion between two species
primary succession on bare rock

explanation: Founders carry only a subset of original population alleles, altering frequencies.
common_mistake: Founder effect is drift, not selection.

question_text: A bottleneck event reduces genetic diversity because
answer: surviving individuals carry limited allelic variation into the recovered population

choices.text: surviving individuals carry limited allelic variation into the recovered population
mutation rate drops to zero permanently
all recessive alleles become dominant
gene flow increases from many new migrants always

explanation: Catastrophic reduction in population size randomly eliminates many alleles.
common_mistake: Bottlenecks decrease, not increase, diversity initially.

question_text: Directional selection shifts the phenotype distribution because
answer: individuals at one extreme have higher fitness

choices.text: individuals at one extreme have higher fitness
all phenotypes have exactly equal survival always
only heterozygotes are selected against every generation
mutation stops occurring entirely

explanation: Directional selection favours one extreme trait value, moving the population mean.
common_mistake: Stabilizing selection favours intermediates, not extremes.

question_text: Stabilizing selection is common for traits like
answer: human birth weight, where intermediate weights have highest survival

choices.text: human birth weight, where intermediate weights have highest survival
peppered moth colour during industrial pollution only
antibiotic resistance increasing over every generation without limit
sexual selection for extreme tail length always

explanation: Stabilizing selection reduces phenotypic variance around an optimal intermediate.
common_mistake: Directional selection on moth colour is a classic directional, not stabilizing, example.

question_text: Disruptive selection can lead to
answer: two distinct phenotypic groups when extremes are favoured over intermediates

choices.text: two distinct phenotypic groups when extremes are favoured over intermediates
a single peak shifting in one direction only
complete Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in one generation
loss of all genetic variation immediately

explanation: Disruptive selection favours both extremes, potentially driving speciation.
common_mistake: Directional selection shifts one peak; disruptive splits into two.

question_text: Speciation through allopatric isolation occurs when
answer: geographic barriers separate populations that diverge genetically

choices.text: geographic barriers separate populations that diverge genetically
species interbreed freely in the same habitat without barriers
gene flow increases between two subpopulations
all mutations are immediately lethal

explanation: Physical separation prevents gene flow, allowing independent evolution.
common_mistake: Sympatric speciation occurs without geographic separation.

question_text: In a community, predation differs from parasitism because predation typically
answer: kills the prey individual directly for consumption

choices.text: kills the prey individual directly for consumption
always benefits both species equally over time
never involves one organism living on or in another
only occurs between plants and fungi

explanation: Predators usually kill prey; parasites often feed without immediate host death.
common_mistake: Parasitism usually does not kill host immediately.

question_text: Mutualism is an interaction where
answer: both species benefit from the association

choices.text: both species benefit from the association
one benefits and the other is harmed
one benefits and the other is unaffected
both species are harmed equally

explanation: Mutualism (+/+) benefits both participants (e.g., mycorrhizae and plants).
common_mistake: Commensalism is +/0; parasitism is +/-.

question_text: Commensalism is characterized by
answer: one organism benefiting and the other unaffected

choices.text: one organism benefiting and the other unaffected
both organisms being harmed
both organisms benefiting equally always
one organism killing the other immediately

explanation: Commensalism (+/0): one benefits, one neutral (e.g., barnacles on whales).
common_mistake: Mutualism requires benefit to both.

question_text: Interspecific competition occurs when
answer: two species require the same limited resource

choices.text: two species require the same limited resource
members of the same species fight only within a clutch
predators always benefit prey populations
commensal species exchange nutrients equally

explanation: Different species competing for shared resources can limit each other's growth (competitive exclusion principle).
common_mistake: Intraspecific competition is within a species.

question_text: A keystone species has disproportionate impact because
answer: its removal causes major community structure changes

choices.text: its removal causes major community structure changes
it is always the most abundant species numerically
it never interacts with other trophic levels
it only exists at the bottom of food chains

explanation: Keystone species (e.g., sea otters) maintain community balance despite lower biomass.
common_mistake: Abundance does not define keystone status.

question_text: Primary succession begins on
answer: substrate with no soil, such as bare rock after volcanic eruption

choices.text: substrate with no soil, such as bare rock after volcanic eruption
previously forested soil with existing seed bank
agricultural fields abandoned for one season only
coral reefs with established diverse communities

explanation: Primary succession builds soil and communities from scratch on lifeless substrate.
common_mistake: Secondary succession follows disturbance where soil remains.

question_text: Secondary succession after a forest fire progresses faster than primary succession because
answer: soil and seed banks often remain available

choices.text: soil and seed banks often remain available
no sunlight reaches the ground ever
all nutrients are permanently removed from Earth
pioneer species cannot colonize disturbed areas

explanation: Existing soil, nutrients, and propagules accelerate community recovery.
common_mistake: Fires may open canopy increasing light for pioneers.

question_text: A climax community is
answer: a relatively stable end stage of succession in a given climate

choices.text: a relatively stable end stage of succession in a given climate
the first pioneer species to colonize bare rock only
a community that never changes regardless of disturbance
always dominated by a single bacterial species

explanation: Climax communities persist until major disturbance resets succession.
common_mistake: Climax can be disrupted; it is not permanently unchanging.

question_text: Exponential population growth occurs when
answer: resources are unlimited and growth rate is proportional to population size

choices.text: resources are unlimited and growth rate is proportional to population size
carrying capacity limits growth immediately from start
death rate always exceeds birth rate
immigration is zero in all populations always

explanation: Exponential growth shows a J-shaped curve: $dN/dt = rN$.
common_mistake: Logistic growth applies when carrying capacity limits population.

question_text: Logistic growth differs from exponential growth because logistic growth
answer: approaches carrying capacity ($K$) as growth rate slows

choices.text: approaches carrying capacity ($K$) as growth rate slows
continues indefinitely without any upper limit
has zero birth rate at all times
ignores environmental resistance factors

explanation: Logistic model: $dN/dt = rN(1-N/K)$ produces S-shaped curve leveling at $K$.
common_mistake: Exponential growth has no carrying capacity term.

question_text: Carrying capacity ($K$) represents
answer: the maximum population size an environment can sustain long-term

choices.text: the maximum population size an environment can sustain long-term
the minimum population before extinction always
the birth rate minus immigration only
the number of predators in a food web exclusively

explanation: $K$ reflects resource limits including food, space, and nesting sites.
common_mistake: Carrying capacity is environmental limit, not minimum viable population.

question_text: A survivorship Type I curve (high survival early, mortality later) is typical of
answer: large mammals with few offspring and high parental care

choices.text: large mammals with few offspring and high parental care
fish producing thousands of eggs with high early mortality
insects with constant mortality rate across ages
bacteria dividing exponentially without aging

explanation: Type I: humans, elephants — most survive to old age then die.
common_mistake: Type III shows high early mortality (many fish, plants).

question_text: r-selected species typically have
answer: many offspring, little parental care, and rapid maturation

choices.text: many offspring, little parental care, and rapid maturation
few offspring with extensive parental investment
very slow population growth always at carrying capacity
extremely long lifespans with one offspring per lifetime always

explanation: r-strategists maximize reproductive rate in unstable environments.
common_mistake: K-selected species have few offspring and high care.

question_text: K-selected species are most likely to thrive when
answer: population is near carrying capacity in a stable environment

choices.text: population is near carrying capacity in a stable environment
environment fluctuates wildly with unlimited resources always
no competition exists for any resource
mutation rate is zero in all organisms

explanation: K-strategists compete efficiently at high density near $K$.
common_mistake: r-selection favours unstable, not stable, high-resource conditions.

skill_tested: Calculating homozygous recessive frequency from allele frequency
question_text: In a population, allele A has frequency $p=0.6$. What is the expected frequency of genotype aa if Hardy-Weinberg applies? Express as decimal to two places.
common_mistake: Students calculate $2pq$ (heterozygote) instead of $q^2$.

question_text: A population grows from $500$ to $680$ individuals in one year. What is the net growth in individuals? Record the three-digit answer.
explanation: $680 - 500 = 180$ individuals.
common_mistake: Students record final population size instead of change.

question_text: Explain how gene flow, genetic drift, and natural selection can each cause allele frequency changes in a population, providing one example of each.
answer: Gene flow: migration introduces new alleles (e.g., pollen from distant plants). Genetic drift: random allele loss in small populations (e.g., founder effect on an island). Natural selection: differential survival/reproduction (e.g., antibiotic resistance in bacteria). All change gene pool frequencies over generations.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Gene flow defined with example — 2 marks. (2) Genetic drift defined with example — 2 marks. (3) Natural selection defined with example — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students describe evolution generally without distinguishing mechanisms.

skill_tested: Applying trophic cascade reasoning to ecosystem disturbance
question_text: A lake ecosystem loses its top predator due to overfishing. Predict two ecological changes that may follow and explain them using community ecology concepts.
answer: Without predators, mesopredator or herbivore populations may increase (trophic cascade), reducing primary producers or smaller prey. Competitive relationships may shift as former prey species increase. Biodiversity may decline if one species dominates (competitive exclusion) or increase temporarily before re-equilibration.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) First predicted change with mechanism — 3 marks. (2) Second predicted change with mechanism — 3 marks. Must reference ecological concepts (trophic cascade, competition, etc.).
common_mistake: Students predict changes without linking to population or community interactions.

question_text: Biotic potential refers to
answer: the maximum reproductive capacity under ideal conditions

choices.text: the maximum reproductive capacity under ideal conditions
the number of predators in an ecosystem only
the rate of soil formation on bare rock
the total biomass of decomposers exclusively

explanation: Biotic potential is theoretical maximum growth rate with unlimited resources.
common_mistake: Carrying capacity is environmental limit, not biotic potential.

question_text: Density-dependent factors regulating population include
answer: competition for food and disease transmission

choices.text: competition for food and disease transmission
volcanic eruptions affecting all populations equally regardless of size
random lightning strikes on individual trees scattered sparsely
solar flares with no biological interaction

explanation: Density-dependent factors intensify as population density increases.
common_mistake: Catastrophic abiotic events are often density-independent.

question_text: Density-independent factors include
answer: severe drought or wildfire affecting population regardless of size

choices.text: severe drought or wildfire affecting population regardless of size
territorial aggression increasing with crowding
parasite spread proportional to host density
competition for nesting sites as population grows

explanation: Density-independent factors impact proportionally at any population size.
common_mistake: Territoriality increases with density — density-dependent.

question_text: Immigration and emigration affect population size by
answer: changing the number of individuals entering or leaving the population

choices.text: changing the number of individuals entering or leaving the population
altering mutation rates in DNA polymerase
changing chromosome number during meiosis
eliminating the need for carrying capacity

explanation: Population change: births + immigration − deaths − emigration.
common_mistake: Demographics affect N; they do not change meiotic chromosome number.

skill_tested: Comparing food webs and food chains
question_text: A food web is more realistic than a food chain because it
answer: shows multiple interconnected feeding relationships

choices.text: shows multiple interconnected feeding relationships
includes only producers and one herbivore
ignores decomposers completely always
assumes energy increases at each trophic level

explanation: Food webs depict complex feeding links among many species.
common_mistake: Energy decreases (about 10% rule) at higher trophic levels.

skill_tested: Explaining energy transfer efficiency between trophic levels
question_text: Approximately 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels because
answer: most energy is lost as heat through cellular respiration and incomplete consumption

choices.text: most energy is lost as heat through cellular respiration and incomplete consumption
producers create energy de novo from nothing
all energy is recycled with 100% efficiency
decomposers return 90% energy to carnivores directly

explanation: Second law of thermodynamics: energy dissipates as heat at each transfer.
common_mistake: Energy is not created by producers from nothing; it is converted from sunlight.

question_text: Biomagnification of DDT in apex predators occurs because
answer: persistent toxins accumulate at higher concentrations up the food chain

choices.text: persistent toxins accumulate at higher concentrations up the food chain
producers metabolize all toxins completely before any transfer
toxins become less concentrated at each higher trophic level
decomposers prevent any chemical from entering food webs

explanation: Lipophilic persistent pollutants concentrate in top consumers.
common_mistake: Biomagnification increases, not decreases, concentration upward.

question_text: Invasive species often disrupt communities because they
answer: lack natural predators and may outcompete native species

choices.text: lack natural predators and may outcompete native species
always increase biodiversity in every ecosystem
immediately form mutualistic relationships with all natives
reduce their own growth rate in new habitats voluntarily

explanation: Invasives can dominate resources and alter habitats, reducing native fitness.
common_mistake: Invasives often decrease, not increase, native biodiversity.

question_text: Edge effect in fragmented habitats can
answer: alter species composition and increase predation or parasitism along boundaries

choices.text: alter species composition and increase predation or parasitism along boundaries
have no impact on interior forest species ever
only benefit deep-interior specialist species
eliminate all abiotic factors from ecosystems

explanation: Habitat edges expose interior species to different microclimate and predators.
common_mistake: Edge effects change community structure; they are not neutral.

question_text: Nitrogen fixation by bacteria benefits ecosystems by
answer: converting atmospheric N$_2$ to ammonia usable by plants

choices.text: converting atmospheric N$_2$ to ammonia usable by plants
removing all nitrogen from soil permanently
producing oxygen from nitrogen gas directly
preventing any decomposition in soil

explanation: Nitrogen fixers (e.g., Rhizobium) enrich soil nitrogen for producers.
common_mistake: Nitrogen fixation adds usable nitrogen; it does not remove it.

question_text: Mycorrhizal fungi form mutualistic associations with plant roots by
answer: extending nutrient absorption while receiving carbohydrates from the plant

choices.text: extending nutrient absorption while receiving carbohydrates from the plant
killing root cells to parasitize all stored starch
fixing atmospheric nitrogen without any plant involvement always
preventing plants from absorbing any water

explanation: Fungi increase phosphorus uptake; plants supply sugars — mutualism.
common_mistake: Mycorrhizae are mutualistic, not purely parasitic on roots.

question_text: Camouflage in prey species is an example of an adaptation resulting from
answer: natural selection favouring individuals matching their environment

choices.text: natural selection favouring individuals matching their environment
genetic drift in infinitely large populations only
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium without any evolution
commensalism where predators are unaffected

explanation: Predation pressure selects cryptic coloration in prey.
common_mistake: Camouflage is adaptation from selection, not drift in infinite populations.

skill_tested: Interpreting industrial melanism as selection example
question_text: Industrial melanism in peppered moths demonstrated
answer: directional selection favouring dark morphs on soot-covered trees

choices.text: directional selection favouring dark morphs on soot-covered trees
stabilizing selection for intermediate pink moths only
no change in allele frequencies over time
complete loss of all genetic variation in one generation

explanation: Dark moths had survival advantage during industrial pollution era — directional selection.
common_mistake: This is classic directional, not stabilizing, selection.

skill_tested: Applying natural selection to antibiotic resistance
question_text: Antibiotic resistance in bacteria spreading through a hospital illustrates
answer: natural selection acting on heritable variation in short time frames

choices.text: natural selection acting on heritable variation in short time frames
Lamarckian inheritance of acquired traits without genes
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in a closed system
complete absence of mutation in bacterial populations

explanation: Resistant bacteria survive antibiotic treatment and reproduce preferentially.
common_mistake: Resistance involves genetic variation and selection, not acquired inheritance only.

question_text: Mark-recapture population estimation uses the proportion of marked individuals in a second sample to estimate total population size.
answer: proportion of marked in recapture sample equals proportion marked in total population

choices.text: proportion of marked in recapture sample equals proportion marked in total population
only birth rate divided by death rate
carrying capacity multiplied by growth rate exclusively
allele frequency times heterozygosity only

explanation: Lincoln-Petersen method: if 10% of recaptured animals are marked, marked release represents 10% of total population.
common_mistake: Mark-recapture estimates population size, not allele frequency directly.

question_text: A J-shaped growth curve on a graph indicates
answer: exponential growth with resources not yet limiting

choices.text: exponential growth with resources not yet limiting
population at carrying capacity with zero growth
immediate population crash to extinction always
logistic growth leveling at K from the start

explanation: J-curve shows accelerating growth characteristic of exponential phase.
common_mistake: S-curve indicates logistic approach to K.

question_text: An S-shaped growth curve indicates
answer: logistic growth approaching carrying capacity

choices.text: logistic growth approaching carrying capacity
exponential growth without limits forever
population declining linearly to zero always
no births occurring throughout the time period

explanation: S-curve: rapid growth slows as $N$ approaches $K$.
common_mistake: J-curve, not S-curve, shows unchecked exponential growth.

question_text: Per capita growth rate $r$ is defined as
answer: birth rate minus death rate per individual per unit time

choices.text: birth rate minus death rate per individual per unit time
total population size divided by carrying capacity only
number of species in a community
energy transfer between trophic levels

explanation: $r$ measures per-individual contribution to population change.
common_mistake: Per capita rate differs from absolute population change.

skill_tested: Describing population dynamics at carrying capacity
question_text: A population at carrying capacity has growth rate approximately
answer: zero (births ≈ deaths)

choices.text: zero (births ≈ deaths)
maximally exponential with no limiting factors
negative always leading to immediate extinction
infinite due to unlimited resources

explanation: At $K$, environmental resistance balances reproduction; net growth nears zero.
common_mistake: Exponential growth occurs far below $K$ with ample resources.

question_text: Ecological footprint measures
answer: human demand on ecosystem resources and waste absorption relative to Earth's capacity

choices.text: human demand on ecosystem resources and waste absorption relative to Earth's capacity
only the number of species in a rainforest
the rate of DNA replication in human cells
action potential frequency in motor neurons

explanation: Footprint quantifies sustainability of consumption patterns.
common_mistake: Ecological footprint is resource accounting, not cellular physiology.

question_text: Sustainable resource management aims to
answer: meet present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs

choices.text: meet present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs
exploit all resources immediately for maximum short-term profit only
eliminate all human technology from ecosystems
prevent any change in community composition ever

explanation: Sustainability balances use and regeneration for long-term ecosystem health.
common_mistake: Unlimited exploitation contradicts sustainability principles.

question_text: Order ecological succession stages on bare rock: (1) lichens and mosses, (2) climax forest, (3) grasses and shrubs, (4) small trees. Record all four digits.
explanation: Pioneer lichens/mosses (1), grasses/shrubs (3), small trees (4), climax forest (2).
common_mistake: Climax placed before pioneer species.

question_text: If $p=0.7$ for allele B, what is frequency of heterozygotes under Hardy-Weinberg? Express as decimal to two places.
common_mistake: Students calculate $p^2$ or $q^2$ instead.

question_text: Population $N_0=1200$, $N_1=1450$ after one year. Per capita growth rate $r$? Express as decimal to two places.

skill_tested: Analyzing human impact and mitigation on biodiversity
question_text: Describe three ways human activities can reduce biodiversity in Alberta ecosystems and propose one scientifically sound mitigation strategy for each.
answer: Habitat fragmentation from agriculture/urbanization reduces gene flow and increases edge effects — mitigate with wildlife corridors. Pollution (e.g., nutrient runoff) causes eutrophication — mitigate with buffer zones and wastewater treatment. Overexploitation of species (overfishing/hunting) — mitigate with quotas based on population surveys.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Three distinct human impacts — 3 marks. (2) One valid mitigation per impact — 3 marks. Strategies must be scientifically plausible.
common_mistake: Students list impacts without feasible mitigation strategies.

question_text: The nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the
answer: myelin sheath where action potentials are regenerated

synaptic cleft between two neurons
nuclear envelope during mitosis

explanation: Myelin gaps expose voltage-gated channels for impulse regeneration in saltatory conduction.

question_text: GABA is an inhibitory neurotransmitter that typically causes
answer: hyperpolarization of the postsynaptic membrane

choices.text: hyperpolarization of the postsynaptic membrane

release of acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction

question_text: The blood-testis barrier formed by Sertoli cells prevents

chemicals dissolved in blood plasma only

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Describe the process of DNA replication, identifying the roles of helicase, primase, DNA polymerase, and ligase.
answer: Helicase unwinds the double helix at the replication fork. Primase synthesizes short RNA primers providing 3' OH. DNA polymerase III extends new DNA strands 5'→3' on leading and lagging strands (Okazaki fragments). DNA polymerase I replaces primers with DNA. Ligase joins Okazaki fragments into continuous lagging strand.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Helicase — 1 mark. (2) Primase — 1 mark. (3) DNA polymerase — 2 marks. (4) Ligase — 1 mark. (5) Leading/lagging distinction — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students omit Okazaki fragments or ligase function.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A couple with normal vision has a colour-blind son. Explain how this pattern is consistent with X-linked recessive inheritance and state the mother's likely genotype.
answer: Colour blindness is X-linked recessive. Affected son (X$^c$Y) inherits X$^c$ from mother and Y from father. Father is normal (X$^N$Y) so cannot pass X$^c$. Mother must be carrier X$^N$X$^c$ (normal vision but passes X$^c$ to 50% of sons).
explanation: Marking guide: (1) X-linked recessive identified — 2 marks. (2) Son inherits X from mother — 1 mark. (3) Mother carrier genotype — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students claim father passes colour blindness to son via Y chromosome.

skill_tested: Integrating ADH in water balance homeostasis
question_text: Explain how the human body maintains water balance on a hot day when sweating increases, referencing ADH and at least one target organ.
answer: Sweating increases water loss, raising blood osmolarity. Osmoreceptors in hypothalamus detect change, increasing ADH release from posterior pituitary. ADH acts on kidney collecting ducts, inserting aquaporins to reabsorb more water, producing concentrated urine. Thirst centre also promotes fluid intake. Negative feedback restores osmolarity toward set point.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Increased osmolarity from sweating — 1 mark. (2) ADH release pathway — 2 marks. (3) Kidney collecting duct effect — 2 marks. (4) Negative feedback — 1 mark.
common_mistake: Students describe sweating but omit ADH renal mechanism.

question_text: Explain how negative feedback regulates thyroid hormone levels. Include TRH, TSH, T3/T4, and the glands involved.
answer: Hypothalamus releases TRH → anterior pituitary releases TSH → thyroid releases T3/T4. Elevated T3/T4 inhibits TRH and TSH (negative feedback), stabilizing levels.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) TRH/TSH pathway — 2 marks. (2) T3/T4 source — 1 mark. (3) Negative feedback inhibition — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students list hormones without feedback direction.

question_text: Compare rod and cone cells in terms of light sensitivity, colour detection, and distribution in the retina.
answer: Rods: highly sensitive in dim light, no colour, abundant peripherally. Cones: less sensitive, detect colour, concentrated in fovea for acuity in bright light.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Light sensitivity contrast — 2 marks. (2) Colour function — 2 marks. (3) Distribution — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students claim rods detect colour.

question_text: Describe hormonal changes during the luteal phase and explain what happens if fertilization does not occur.
answer: After ovulation, corpus luteum secretes progesterone and estrogen, maintaining endometrium. Without fertilization, corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone drops, endometrium sloughs (menstruation), and new follicular phase begins.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Corpus luteum hormones — 2 marks. (2) Endometrial maintenance — 1 mark. (3) Menstruation trigger — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students confuse follicular and luteal phases.

question_text: Explain why teratogen exposure during organogenesis can cause permanent birth defects while the same exposure after birth may have less severe effects.
answer: Organogenesis involves rapid differentiation and pattern formation; disrupting signalling causes irreversible structural errors. After organs mature, cells are more differentiated and less plastic, so equivalent damage is often repairable or localized.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Organogenesis as critical period — 2 marks. (2) Differentiation/plasticity concept — 2 marks. (3) Contrast with mature tissue — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students state all teratogen effects are identical at any age.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Compare crossing over and independent assortment as sources of genetic variation in meiosis.
answer: Crossing over exchanges alleles between homologous chromosomes during prophase I, creating recombinant chromatids. Independent assortment randomizes homologue orientation at metaphase I, creating novel chromosome combinations. Both increase gamete diversity.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Crossing over mechanism — 2 marks. (2) Independent assortment mechanism — 2 marks. (3) Variation outcome for both — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students use the terms interchangeably without distinction.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Explaining tumour suppressor role in cancer
question_text: Explain how a mutation in a tumour suppressor gene such as p53 can contribute to cancer development.
answer: p53 normally pauses cell cycle for DNA repair or triggers apoptosis when damage is detected. Loss-of-function mutations allow damaged cells to divide, accumulating additional mutations and leading to uncontrolled proliferation.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Normal p53 function — 2 marks. (2) Effect of mutation — 2 marks. (3) Link to cancer progression — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students describe p53 as an oncogene that speeds division when active.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: Distinguish between a point mutation, a frameshift mutation, and a chromosomal deletion, giving one consequence of each.
answer: Point mutation: single base change; may be silent, missense, or nonsense. Frameshift: insertions/deletions not in multiples of three alter reading frame downstream. Chromosomal deletion: loss of large segment affecting many genes (e.g., Cri-du-chat).
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Point mutation — 2 marks. (2) Frameshift — 2 marks. (3) Chromosomal deletion — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students define types without stating consequences.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Explaining CRISPR mechanism and STS implications
question_text: Explain how CRISPR-Cas9 can be used to edit a specific gene and discuss one ethical consideration.
answer: Guide RNA directs Cas9 to matching genomic sequence; Cas9 cuts DNA; cell repair pathways introduce desired edit. Ethical issues include off-target effects, germline editing affecting future generations, and equitable access.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Guide RNA targeting — 2 marks. (2) Cas9 cleavage/repair — 2 marks. (3) Valid ethical issue discussed — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students describe PCR instead of CRISPR.

skill_tested: Applying natural selection to antibiotic resistance
question_text: Explain how antibiotic use in agriculture can drive natural selection for antibiotic-resistant bacteria in the environment.
answer: Low-dose antibiotics kill susceptible bacteria, leaving resistant variants that reproduce. Resistance alleles spread through bacterial populations and horizontal gene transfer, creating reservoirs of resistant microbes transmissible to humans.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Selection against susceptible bacteria — 2 marks. (2) Resistant reproduction/gene pool change — 2 marks. (3) Environmental/human health link — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students claim antibiotics cause mutations directly in every bacterium.

question_text: Describe primary succession on a volcanic island and identify the role of pioneer species and climax community.
answer: Bare rock → pioneer lichens/mosses weather substrate and build soil → grasses/shrubs → trees → climax community stable for climate. Pioneers tolerate harsh conditions and enable later species; climax persists until major disturbance.
explanation: Marking guide: (1) Sequence of succession — 2 marks. (2) Pioneer role — 2 marks. (3) Climax definition — 2 marks.
common_mistake: Students confuse primary with secondary succession.

skill_tested: Blocking vesicle fusion in synaptic transmission
question_text: A toxin cleaves SNARE proteins preventing vesicle fusion. Which step of synaptic transmission is blocked?
answer: release of neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft

choices.text: release of neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft
generation of the action potential in the axon
binding of neurotransmitter to postsynaptic receptors
reuptake of neurotransmitter by the presynaptic cell

explanation: SNARE proteins mediate vesicle-membrane fusion during exocytosis.
common_mistake: Students confuse vesicle release with receptor binding.

skill_tested: Predicting steroid hormone mechanism of action
question_text: A steroid hormone enters a target cell and binds a cytoplasmic receptor. The complex directly
answer: acts as a transcription factor altering gene expression

choices.text: acts as a transcription factor altering gene expression
opens ligand-gated ion channels in the plasma membrane
activates adenylyl cyclase to produce cAMP at the membrane
is degraded immediately without entering the nucleus

explanation: Steroid-receptor complexes typically regulate transcription in the nucleus.
common_mistake: Peptide second-messenger pathways are incorrectly applied to steroids.

skill_tested: Evaluating STS implications of chromosomal testing
question_text: Preimplantation genetic testing identifies an embryo carrying a balanced Robertsonian translocation. Parents are informed because
answer: the child may have reduced fertility or produce unbalanced gametes

choices.text: the child may have reduced fertility or produce unbalanced gametes
the embryo will lack all mitochondria from the mother
meiosis will be replaced by mitosis in all adult cells
the translocation guarantees trisomy 21 in every offspring

explanation: Balanced carriers are phenotypically normal but may produce unbalanced gametes during meiosis.
common_mistake: Balanced translocations do not always cause Down syndrome.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Predicting consequences of failed cytokinesis in meiosis I
question_text: A cell completes meiosis I but fails cytokinesis. The immediate result is a cell with
answer: duplicated homologous chromosomes and twice the normal cytoplasm volume

choices.text: duplicated homologous chromosomes and twice the normal cytoplasm volume
haploid unduplicated chromosomes in four separate cells
no DNA content because replication did not occur
triploid chromosome number in one nucleus

explanation: Nuclear division without cytokinesis doubles DNA/cytoplasm without separating cells.
common_mistake: Meiosis I without cytokinesis does not immediately yield haploid cells.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Inferring timing of sex chromosome nondisjunction
question_text: Primary nondisjunction of sex chromosomes producing XXY most likely occurred during
answer: meiosis I in the father producing XY sperm

choices.text: meiosis I in the father producing XY sperm
mitosis in maternal liver cells
DNA replication in the zygote only
cytokinesis of the first embryonic cleavage exclusively

explanation: XXY commonly arises from XY sperm (father meiosis I error) or XX egg; XY sperm implies paternal MI nondisjunction.
common_mistake: Students attribute all sex chromosome aneuploidy to maternal meiosis only.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Converting recombination frequency to map distance
question_text: Two genes show 18% recombination frequency. The map distance between them is
answer: 18 map units

choices.text: 18 map units
36 map units because recombination is doubled in meiosis II
9 map units because only half of chromatids recombine
50 map units indicating independent assortment

explanation: 1% recombination = 1 map unit; 18% = 18 map units.
common_mistake: Map distance equals recombination frequency, not half or double.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Predicting epigenetic silencing of tumour suppressors
question_text: Hypermethylation of a tumour suppressor gene promoter most likely causes
answer: reduced transcription of the tumour suppressor

choices.text: reduced transcription of the tumour suppressor
increased translation of the protein product from mRNA
permanent deletion of the gene from the chromosome
instant frameshift mutation in all exons

explanation: Promoter methylation typically silences gene expression epigenetically.
common_mistake: Methylation represses transcription; it does not increase translation.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: A trinucleotide repeat expansion in a coding region that lengthens a polyglutamine tract often causes disease through
answer: toxic gain-of-function of the altered protein

choices.text: toxic gain-of-function of the altered protein
complete loss of the gene from the genome by deletion
conversion of the gene into a noncoding RNA only
prevention of all DNA replication in somatic cells

explanation: Expanded polyglutamine tracts (e.g., Huntington) create aberrant protein function.
common_mistake: Repeat expansion causes toxic protein, not simple gene absence.

skill_tested: Calculating heterozygote frequency from recessive phenotype data
question_text: In a population in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, 16% show recessive phenotype. The frequency of heterozygotes is
common_mistake: Students report q² or p² instead of 2pq.

skill_tested: Applying per capita growth rate to population size
question_text: A population of 5000 has per capita growth rate r=0.12/year. Approximate growth in one year (individuals) is
common_mistake: Students forget to multiply by population size.

skill_tested: Integrating spinal tract anatomy with lesion deficits
question_text: After spinal cord hemisection, a patient shows loss of voluntary motor control and proprioception on the same side below the lesion, and loss of pain/temperature on the opposite side. The tracts damaged include
answer: corticospinal and dorsal columns ipsilaterally; spinothalamic contralaterally

choices.text: corticospinal and dorsal columns ipsilaterally; spinothalamic contralaterally
only optic radiation fibres bilaterally
all cranial nerves exiting above the lesion only
sympathetic preganglionic fibres without any sensory loss

explanation: Brown-Séquard syndrome reflects tract decussation patterns.
common_mistake: Students expect all deficits on the same side.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: An X-linked dominant disorder affects heterozygous females and hemizygous males. Affected father (X^D Y) × unaffected mother (X^N X^N) produces
answer: all daughters affected, no sons affected

choices.text: all daughters affected, no sons affected
all sons affected, no daughters affected
no affected children of either sex
50% of both sons and daughters affected

explanation: Father passes X^D to all daughters (affected) and Y to sons (unaffected).
common_mistake: Sons inherit Y from father in X-linked patterns.

skill_tested: Evaluating STS goals of therapeutic cloning
question_text: Somatic cell nuclear transfer for therapeutic cloning aims to
answer: produce genetically matched stem cells for potential tissue repair

choices.text: produce genetically matched stem cells for potential tissue repair
create a genetically unique child with two biological fathers only
eliminate the need for meiosis in all human reproduction permanently
replace prenatal genetic screening with mitochondrial sequencing only

explanation: Therapeutic cloning seeks patient-matched cells, raising distinct ethics from reproductive cloning.
common_mistake: Therapeutic cloning is not identical to reproductive cloning goals.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
skill_tested: Interpreting mitotic index with cytokinesis data
question_text: If a culture has mitotic index 10% and 2% of cells are in cytokinesis visually, the proportion in nuclear division (mitosis proper) is approximately

choices.text: 10% including interphase

explanation: Mitotic index counts mitosis; cytokinesis follows telophase — 10% total mitotic cells minus 2% in cytokinesis ≈ 8% in mitosis.
common_mistake: Students add rather than separate cytokinesis from mitosis.

skill_tested: Applying competitive exclusion with resource availability
question_text: Species A competitively excludes species B when resources are limiting, but both coexist when resource availability doubles. This supports
answer: competitive exclusion can be avoided when resources are not limiting

choices.text: competitive exclusion can be avoided when resources are not limiting
competitive exclusion is impossible in all ecosystems always
predation never affects community composition
primary succession cannot occur on disturbed soil

explanation: Coexistence becomes possible when niche overlap is reduced by greater resource availability.
common_mistake: Competitive exclusion depends on resource limitation.

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: In a population 36% show recessive phenotype for a gene. Under Hardy-Weinberg, what is allele frequency $p$ of the dominant allele? Express as decimal to two places.
common_mistake: Students use 0.36 as p instead of q².

unit: Cell Division, Genetics, and Molecular Biology
question_text: An X-linked recessive allele has frequency 0.08 in males. What is female carrier frequency (2pq)? Express as decimal to two places.
explanation: Males hemizygous: q=0.08, p=0.92; 2pq=0.147≈0.15.
common_mistake: Students forget females need 2pq for carriers.

question_text: Recessive phenotype frequency is 4%. What percent of population is heterozygous? Express as whole number percent.
common_mistake: Students report 4% or 64%.

question_text: Population grows from 2000 to 2600 in 4 years. Average annual per capita growth rate? Express as decimal to two places.
explanation: Total fractional growth (2600-2000)/2000=0.3 over 4 years; r≈0.3/4=0.075≈0.07.
common_mistake: Students divide by 2600 or forget to divide by years.
